revision.comment: Created by translating the page "[[:ja:Special:Redirect/revision/85661678|クマノクスビ]]"
revision.text:
[[File:Challenge of Amaterasu and Susanoo-eng.svg|thumb|240x240px|Oath between Amaterasu and Susanowo (based on the Kojiki) View in SVG (supported browsers only)]] '''クマノクスビ'''は、[[Japanese mythology|日本神話]]に登場する[[Kami|神]]である。
== 概要 == 誓約の段において、『[[Kojiki|古事記]]』、『[[Nihon Shoki|日本書紀]]』において[[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|須佐之男命]]が[[Amaterasu|天照大御神]]の持ち物である八尺勾璁を譲り受けて化生させた五柱（『日本書紀』第三の一書では六柱）の神の一柱で、天照大御神の物実から生まれたので天照大御神の子であると宣言された。
『古事記』では'''熊野久須毘命'''、『日本書紀』本文では'''熊野櫲樟日命'''（クマノクスヒ）、第一の一書では'''熊野忍蹈命'''（クマノオシホミ）、第二の一書では'''熊野櫲樟日命'''（クマノクスヒ）、第三の一書では'''熊野忍蹈命'''（クマノオシホミ）またの名を'''熊野忍隅命'''（クマノオシスミ）、別段（[[Amano-Iwato|岩戸隠れ]]）第三の一書では'''熊野大角命'''（クマノオオスミ）と表記されている。また'''[[Ame-no-hohi|熊野大隅命]]'''（クマノオオスミ）とも。いずれも最後（5番目または6番目）に化生した神とされている。
== 熊野との関連 == The deity's name, Kusubi (Kusuhi), is thought to mean "strange spirit" (mysterious divine spirit) or "strange fire.「クマノ」は[[Kumano Region|熊野]]のことであり、出雲の熊野大社（[[Shimane Prefecture|島根県]][[Matsue|松江市]]）のこととも、紀伊の熊野三山のことともされる。The current deity of Kumano-taisha is "Kumano-taishin Kushimikino-no-mikoto," but there is a theory that the original deity was Kumanokusubi.
熊野三山の一つの[[Kumano Nachi Taisha|熊野那智大社]]（[[Wakayama Prefecture|和歌山県]][[Higashimuro District, Wakayama|東牟婁郡]][[Nachikatsuura|那智勝浦町]]）の祭神・熊野夫須美大神は[[Izanami|伊弉冉尊]]のこととされるが、これもクマノクスビのことであるとする説がある。<ref>{{Cite book|title=『日本の神様読み解き事典』|publisher=柏書房|others=川口謙二編著|year=1999|isbn=978-4-7601-1824-3|和書}}</ref>
== 脚注 == {{Reflist}} [[Category:Japanese gods]]

[[File:Challenge of Amaterasu and Susanoo-eng.svg|thumb|240x240px|Oath between Amaterasu and Susanowo (based on the Kojiki) View in SVG (supported browsers only)]]
'''Kumanokusubi''' is a [[Kami|God]] in [[Japanese mythology|Japanese mythology]].
== Summary == In [[Kojiki|Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki]], he is one of the five (or six in the third book of [[Nihon Shoki]]) deities that [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] incarnated after receiving the eight-shaku-tsunami that belonged to [[Amaterasu]].
He was declared to be the son of Amaterasu because he was born from the fruit of Amaterasu's body. ''Kumano Kusubi-no-mikoto'' in the Kojiki (Records of Ancient Matters), ''Kumano Kamanokusuhi-no-mikoto'' in the main text of the Nihongi (Chronicles of Japan), ''Kumano Oshihomi-no-mikoto'' in the first book, ''Kumano Kamanokusuhi-no-mikoto'' in the second book, ''Kumano Kamanokusuhi-no-mikoto'' in the third book, and ''Kumano Kusuhi-no-mikoto'' in the fourth book. In the second book, he is referred to as '''Kumano Kusuhi''', in the third book as '''Kumano Oshikomi''', and in the third book as '''Kumano Oshikumi''', and in the third book as '''[[Amano-Iwato|Iwato-Kagakure]]'''. In the third book, he is called '''Kumano-ohkaku-no-mikoto''' (Kumano-ohsumi). Both are said to be the last (5th or 6th) incarnations of the deity.
== Related to Kumano ==
The deity's name, Kusubi (Kusuhi), is thought to mean "strange spirit" (mysterious divine spirit) or "strange fire.「クマノ」は[[Kumano Region|熊野]]のことであり、出雲の熊野大社（[[Shimane Prefecture|島根県]][[Matsue|松江市]]）のこととも、紀伊の熊野三山のことともされる。The current deity of Kumano-taisha is "Kumano-taishin Kushimikino-no-mikoto," but there is a theory that the original deity was Kumanokusubi.
熊野三山の一つの[[Kumano Nachi Taisha|熊野那智大社]]（[[Wakayama Prefecture|和歌山県]][[Higashimuro District, Wakayama|東牟婁郡]][[Nachikatsuura|那智勝浦町]]）の祭神・熊野夫須美大神は[[Izanami|伊弉冉尊]]のこととされるが、これもクマノクスビのことであるとする説がある。<ref>{{Cite book|title=『日本の神様読み解き事典』|publisher=柏書房|others=川口謙二編著|year=1999|isbn=978-4-7601-1824-3|和書}}</ref>
== 脚注 == {{Reflist}} [[Category:Japanese gods]]

[[File:Challenge of Amaterasu and Susanoo-eng.svg|thumb|240x240px|Oath between Amaterasu and Susanowo (based on the Kojiki) View in SVG (supported browsers only)]]
'''Kumanokusubi''' is a [[Kami|God]] in [[Japanese mythology|Japanese mythology]].
== Summary == In [[Kojiki|Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki]], he is one of the five (or six in the third book of [[Nihon Shoki]]) deities that [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] incarnated after receiving the eight-shaku-tsunami that belonged to [[Amaterasu]].
He was declared to be the son of Amaterasu because he was born from the fruit of Amaterasu's body. ''Kumano Kusubi-no-mikoto'' in the Kojiki (Records of Ancient Matters), ''Kumano Kamanokusuhi-no-mikoto'' in the main text of the Nihongi (Chronicles of Japan), ''Kumano Oshihomi-no-mikoto'' in the first book, ''Kumano Kamanokusuhi-no-mikoto'' in the second book, ''Kumano Kamanokusuhi-no-mikoto'' in the third book, and ''Kumano Kusuhi-no-mikoto'' in the fourth book. In the second book, he is referred to as '''Kumano Kusuhi''', in the third book as '''Kumano Oshikomi''', and in the third book as '''Kumano Oshikumi''', and in the third book as '''[[Amano-Iwato|Iwato-Kagakure]]'''. In the third book, he is called '''Kumano-ohkaku-no-mikoto''' (Kumano-ohsumi). Both are said to be the last (5th or 6th) incarnations of the deity.
== Related to Kumano ==
The deity's name, Kusubi (Kusuhi), is thought to mean "strange spirit" (mysterious divine spirit) or "strange fire. The current deity of Kumano-taisha is "Kumano-taishin Kushimikino- The current deity of Kumano-taisha is "Kumano-taishin Kushimikino- no-mikoto," but there is a theory that the original deity was Kumanokusubi.
Kumano-taishin Kushimikino- no-mikoto," but there is a theory that the original deity was Kumanokusubi. There is a theory that the deity of [[Nachikatsuura Town]], [[Kumanokusubi]], is [[Izanami|Izamiami]], but this is also believed to be a reference to Kumanokusubi. <ref>{{Cite book|title="Nihon no kami yomi kakkai jiten"|publisher=Kashiwa Shobo|others=Kawaguchi Kenji (ed.)|year=1999|isbn=978-4-7601-1824-3|Japanese book}}</ref>}

[[File:Challenge of Amaterasu and Susanoo-eng.svg|thumb|240x240px|Oath between Amaterasu and Susanowo (based on the Kojiki) View in SVG (supported browsers only)]]
'''Kumanokusubi''' is a [[Kami|God]] in [[Japanese mythology|Japanese mythology]]. He is the fifth son of [[Amaterasu]].<ref>{{Cite web|title=Kumanokusubi • A History of Japan - 日本歴史|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/kumanokusubi/|access-date=2021-09-28|website=A History of Japan - 日本歴史|language=en-GB}}</ref>
Some scholars have identified this ''[[kami]]'' as the ''saijin'' at the shrine [[Kumano Jinja]] in Shimane Prefecture.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp//eos/detail/id=9369|url-status=live|archive-url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9369|access-date=2021-09-28|website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|language=ja}}</ref>
== Name == He goes by other names like '''Kumano no oshihomi no mikoto''', '''Kumano no oshikuma no mikoto''', '''Kumano no oshisumi no mikoto''', and '''Kumano no osumi no mikoto.'''<ref name=":0" />
== Summary == In [[Kojiki|Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki]], he is one of the five (or six in the third book of [[Nihon Shoki]]) deities that [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] incarnated after receiving the eight-shaku-tsunami that belonged to [[Amaterasu]].
He was declared to be the son of Amaterasu because he was born from the fruit of Amaterasu's body. ''Kumano Kusubi-no-mikoto'' in the Kojiki (Records of Ancient Matters), ''Kumano Kamanokusuhi-no-mikoto'' in the main text of the Nihongi (Chronicles of Japan), ''Kumano Oshihomi-no-mikoto'' in the first book, ''Kumano Kamanokusuhi-no-mikoto'' in the second book, ''Kumano Kamanokusuhi-no-mikoto'' in the third book, and ''Kumano Kusuhi-no-mikoto'' in the fourth book. In the second book, he is referred to as '''Kumano Kusuhi''', in the third book as '''Kumano Oshikomi''', and in the third book as '''Kumano Oshikumi''', and in the third book as '''[[Amano-Iwato|Iwato-Kagakure]]'''. In the third book, he is called '''Kumano-ohkaku-no-mikoto''' (Kumano-ohsumi). Both are said to be the last (5th or 6th) incarnations of the deity.
== Related to Kumano ==
The deity's name, Kusubi (Kusuhi), is thought to mean "strange spirit" (mysterious divine spirit) or "strange fire. The current deity of Kumano-taisha is "Kumano-taishin Kushimikino- The current deity of Kumano-taisha is "Kumano-taishin Kushimikino- no-mikoto," but there is a theory that the original deity was Kumanokusubi.
Kumano-taishin Kushimikino- no-mikoto," but there is a theory that the original deity was Kumanokusubi. There is a theory that the deity of [[Kumano Nachi Taisha]], [[Kumanokusubi]], is [[Izanami|Izamiami]], but this is also believed to be a reference to Kumanokusubi. <ref>{{Cite book|title="Nihon no kami yomi kakkai jiten"|publisher=Kashiwa Shobo|others=Kawaguchi Kenji (ed.)|year=1999|isbn=978-4-7601-1824-3|Japanese book}}</ref>}

[[File:Challenge of Amaterasu and Susanoo-eng.svg|thumb|240x240px|Oath between Amaterasu and Susanowo (based on the Kojiki) View in SVG (supported browsers only)]]

Some scholars have identified this ''[[kami]]'' as the ''saijin'' at the shrine [[Kumano Jinja]] in Shimane Prefecture.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp//eos/detail/id=9369|url-status=live|archive-url=|access-date=2021-09-28|website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|language=ja}}</ref>

''Kumano Kusubi-no-mikoto'' in the Kojiki (Records of Ancient Matters), ''Kumano Kamanokusuhi-no-mikoto'' in the main text of the Nihongi (Chronicles of Japan), ''Kumano Oshihomi-no-mikoto'' in the first book, ''Kumano Kamanokusuhi-no-mikoto'' in the second book, ''Kumano Kamanokusuhi-no-mikoto'' in the third book, and ''Kumano Kusuhi-no-mikoto'' in the fourth book. In the second book, he is referred to as '''Kumano Kusuhi''', in the third book as '''Kumano Oshikomi''', and in the third book as '''Kumano Oshikumi''', and in the third book as '''[[Amano-Iwato|Iwato-Kagakure]]'''. In the third book, he is called '''Kumano-ohkaku-no-mikoto''' (Kumano-ohsumi). Both are said to be the last (5th or 6th) incarnations of the deity.
== Related to Kumano ==
The deity's name, Kusubi (Kusuhi), is thought to mean "strange spirit" (mysterious divine spirit) or "strange fire. The current deity of Kumano-taisha is "Kumano-taishin Kushimikino- The current deity of Kumano-taisha is "Kumano-taishin Kushimikino- no-mikoto," but there is a theory that the original deity was Kumanokusubi.
Kumano-taishin Kushimikino- no-mikoto," but there is a theory that the original deity was Kumanokusubi. There is a theory that the deity of [[Kumano Nachi Taisha]], [[Kumanokusubi]], is [[Izanami|Izamiami]], but this is also believed to be a reference to Kumanokusubi. <ref>{{Cite book|title="Nihon no kami yomi kakkai jiten"|publisher=Kashiwa Shobo|others=Kawaguchi Kenji (ed.)|year=1999|isbn=978-4-7601-1824-3|language=ja}}</ref>}

''Kumano Kusubi-no-mikoto'' in the Kojiki (Records of Ancient Matters), ''Kumano Kamanokusuhi-no-mikoto'' in the main text of the Nihongi (Chronicles of Japan), ''Kumano Oshihomi-no-mikoto'' in the first book, ''Kumano Kamanokusuhi-no-mikoto'' in the second book, ''Kumano Kamanokusuhi-no-mikoto'' in the third book, and ''Kumano Kusuhi-no-mikoto'' in the fourth book. In the second book, he is referred to as '''Kumano Kusuhi''', in the third book as '''Kumano Oshikomi''', and in the third book as '''Kumano Oshikumi''', and in the third book as '''[[Amano-Iwato|Iwato-Kagakure]]'''. In the third book, he is called '''Kumano-ohkaku-no-mikoto''' (Kumano-ohsumi). Both are said to be the last (5th or 6th) incarnations of the deity.
== Related to Kumano ==
The deity's name, Kusubi (Kusuhi), is thought to mean "strange spirit" (mysterious divine spirit) or "strange fire. The current deity of Kumano-taisha is "Kumano-taishin Kushimikino- The current deity of Kumano-taisha is "Kumano-taishin Kushimikino- no-mikoto," but there is a theory that the original deity was Kumanokusubi.
Kumano-taishin Kushimikino- no-mikoto," but there is a theory that the original deity was Kumanokusubi. There is a theory that the deity of [[Kumano Nachi Taisha]], Kumanokusubi, is [[Izanami|Izamiami]], but this is also believed to be a reference to Kumanokusubi.<ref>{{Cite book|title="Nihon no kami yomi kakkai jiten"|publisher=Kashiwa Shobo|others=Kawaguchi Kenji (ed.)|year=1999|isbn=978-4-7601-1824-3|language=ja}}</ref>}

In many versions, Susanoo took Amaterasu's beads and crushed them within his mouth, which created five male kami.<ref>{{cite book |author=Seigo Takahashi |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=H18tAAAAYAAJ&dq=Amaterasu+and+Susanoo+oath&pg=PA66 |title=A Study of the Origin of the Japanese State |publisher=W. Gray |year=1917}}</ref><ref name=":12">{{Cite web |title=Amenooshihomimi • A History of Japan - 日本歴史 |url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/amenooshihomimi/ |access-date=2020-09-28 |website=A History of Japan - 日本歴史 |language=en-GB}}</ref> The first one to be born was [[Ame-no-oshihomimi|Amenooshihomimi]], second was [[Ame-no-hohi]], third was [[Amatsuhikone]], fourth was [[Ikutsuhikone]], and [[Kumanokusubi]] was the fifth.<ref name=":04">{{Cite book |last1=Havens |first1=Norman |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a_YQAQAAIAAJ&q=Amenooshihomimi |title=An Encyclopedia of Shinto (Shinto Jiten): Kami |last2=Inoue |first2=Nobutaka |date=2006 |publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics Kokugakuin University |isbn=978-4-905853-08-4 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Shinto Portal - IJCC, Kokugakuin University |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=33}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |year=1990 |title=The Journal of the Association of Teachers of Japanese |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Q5cqAQAAIAAJ&q=Amatsuhikone}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Kumanokusubi |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=99 |access-date=2020-10-07 |website=eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp}}</ref>
== Related to Kumano ==
The deity's name, Kusubi (Kusuhi), is thought to mean "strange spirit" (mysterious divine spirit) or "strange fire. The current deity of Kumano-taisha is "Kumano-taishin Kushimikino- The current deity of Kumano-taisha is "Kumano-taishin Kushimikino- no-mikoto," but there is a theory that the original deity was Kumanokusubi.
Kumano-taishin Kushimikino- no-mikoto," but there is a theory that the original deity was Kumanokusubi. Kumano-taishin Kushimikino- no-mikoto," but there is a theory that the original deity was Kumanokusubi. There is a theory that the deity of [[Kumano Nachi Taisha]], Kumanokusubi, is [[Izanami|Izamiami]], but this is also believed to be a reference to Kumanokusubi.<ref>{{Cite book|title="Nihon no kami yomi kakkai jiten"|publisher=Kashiwa Shobo|others=Kawaguchi Kenji (ed.)|year=1999|isbn=978-4-7601-1824-3|language=ja}}</ref>}
== References == {{Reflist}} [[Category:Japanese gods]] [[Category:Amatsukami]]

{{Short description|God in Japanese mythology}} {{refimprove|date=January 2022}} {{Infobox deity | type = Shinto | name = Kumanokusubi | siblings = [[Ame-no-hohi]] (brother) [[Ame-no-oshihomimi]] (brother) [[Ikutsuhikone]] (brother) [[Amatsuhikone]] (brother) | mother = [[Amaterasu]] }}[[File:Challenge of Amaterasu and Susanoo-eng.svg|thumb|240x240px|Oath between Amaterasu and Susanowo (based on the Kojiki)]]
'''Kumanokusubi''' (熊野久须毘命,熊野櫲樟日命, Wonder Worker of Bear Moors)<ref name=":1" /> is a [[Kami|God]] in [[Japanese mythology]]. He is the fifth son of [[Amaterasu]].<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|title=Kumanokusubi • A History of Japan - 日本歴史|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/kumanokusubi/|access-date=2021-09-28|website=A History of Japan - 日本歴史|language=en-GB}}</ref><ref>https://archive.today/20230320012745/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9369</ref>
Some scholars have identified this ''[[kami]]'' as the ''saijin'' at the shrine [[Kumano Jinja]] in Shimane Prefecture.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230214183150/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9369 |archive-url= |access-date=2021-09-28 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref>
== Name == He goes by other names like '''Kumano no oshihomi no mikoto''', '''Kumano no oshikuma no mikoto''', '''Kumano no oshisumi no mikoto''', and '''Kumano no osumi no mikoto.'''<ref name=":0" />
== Summary == He was born out of a kami making competition between Amaterasu and [[Susanoo]].
In many versions, Susanoo took Amaterasu's beads and crushed them within his mouth, which created five male kami.<ref>{{cite book |author=Seigo Takahashi |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=H18tAAAAYAAJ&dq=Amaterasu+and+Susanoo+oath&pg=PA66 |title=A Study of the Origin of the Japanese State |publisher=W. D. Gray |year=1917}}</ref><ref name=":12">{{Cite web |title=Amenooshihomimi • A History of Japan - 日本歴史 |url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/amenooshihomimi/ |access-date=2020-09-28 |website=A History of Japan - 日本歴史 |language=en-GB}}</ref> The first one to be born was [[Ame-no-oshihomimi|Amenooshihomimi]], second was [[Ame-no-hohi]], third was [[Amatsuhikone]], fourth was [[Ikutsuhikone]], and [[Kumanokusubi]] was the fifth.<ref name=":04">{{Cite book |last1=Havens |first1=Norman |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a_YQAQAAIAAJ&q=Amenooshihomimi |title=An Encyclopedia of Shinto (Shinto Jiten): Kami |last2=Inoue |first2=Nobutaka |date=2006 |publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics Kokugakuin University |isbn=978-4-905853-08-4 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Shinto Portal - IJCC, Kokugakuin University |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=33}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |year=1990 |title=The Journal of the Association of Teachers of Japanese |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Q5cqAQAAIAAJ&q=Amatsuhikone}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Kumanokusubi |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=99 |access-date=2020-10-07 |website=eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp}}</ref>
== Related to Kumano ==
The deity's name, Kusubi (Kusuhi), is thought to mean "strange spirit" (mysterious divine spirit) or "strange fire. The current deity of Kumano-taisha is "Kumano-taishin Kushimikino- The current deity of Kumano-taisha is "Kumano-taishin Kushimikino- no-mikoto," but there is a theory that the original deity was Kumanokusubi.
Kumano-taishin Kushimikino- no-mikoto," but there is a theory that the original deity was Kumanokusubi. There is a theory that the deity of [[Kumano Nachi Taisha]], Kumanokusubi, is [[Izanami|Izamiami]], but this is also believed to be a reference to Kumanokusubi.<ref>{{Cite book|title="Nihon no kami yomi kakkai jiten"|publisher=Kashiwa Shobo|others=Kawaguchi Kenji (ed.)|year=1999|isbn=978-4-7601-1824-3|language=ja}}</ref>}

Gray |year=1917}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Amenooshihomimi • A History of Japan - 日本歴史 |url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/amenooshihomimi/ |access-date=2020-09-28 |website=A History of Japan - 日本歴史 |language=en-GB}}</ref> The first one to be born was [[Ame-no-oshihomimi|Amenooshihomimi]], second was [[Ame-no-hohi]], third was [[Amatsuhikone]], fourth was [[Ikutsuhikone]], and [[Kumanokusubi]] was the fifth.<ref>{{Cite book |last1=Havens |first1=Norman |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a_YQAQAAIAAJ&q=Amenooshihomimi |title=An Encyclopedia of Shinto (Shinto Jiten): Kami |last2=Inoue |first2=Nobutaka |date=2006 |publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics Kokugakuin University |isbn=978-4-905853-08-4 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Shinto Portal - IJCC, Kokugakuin University |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=33}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |year=1990 |title=The Journal of the Association of Teachers of Japanese |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Q5cqAQAAIAAJ&q=Amatsuhikone}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Kumanokusubi |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=99 |access-date=2020-10-07 |website=eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp}}</ref>
== Related to Kumano ==
The deity's name, Kusubi (Kusuhi), is thought to mean "strange spirit" (mysterious divine spirit) or "strange fire. The current deity of Kumano-taisha is "Kumano-taishin Kushimikino- The current deity of Kumano-taisha is "Kumano-taishin Kushimikino- no-mikoto," but there is a theory that the original deity was Kumanokusubi.
Kumano-taishin Kushimikino- no-mikoto," but there is a theory that the original deity was Kumanokusubi. There is a theory that the deity of [[Kumano Nachi Taisha]], Kumanokusubi, is [[Izanami|Izamiami]], but this is also believed to be a reference to Kumanokusubi.<ref>{{Cite book|title="Nihon no kami yomi kakkai jiten"|publisher=Kashiwa Shobo|others=Kawaguchi Kenji (ed.)|year=1999|isbn=978-4-7601-1824-3|language=ja}}</ref>}

{{Short description|God in Japanese mythology}} {{refimprove|date=January 2022}} {{Infobox deity | type = Shinto | name = Kumanokusubi | siblings = [[Ame-no-hohi]] (brother) [[Ame-no-oshihomimi]] (brother) [[Ikutsuhikone]] (brother) [[Amatsuhikone]] (brother) | mother = [[Amaterasu]] }}[[File:Children born from the Challenge of Amaterasu and Susanoo-en.svg|thumb|240x240px|Oath between Amaterasu and Susanowo (based on the Kojiki)]]
'''Kumanokusubi''' (熊野久须毘命,熊野櫲樟日命, Wonder Worker of Bear Moors)<ref name="a-history-of-japan-日本歴史-2021a" /> is a [[Kami|God]] in [[Japanese mythology]]. He is the fifth son of [[Amaterasu]].<ref name="a-history-of-japan-日本歴史-2021a">{{Cite web|title=Kumanokusubi • A History of Japan - 日本歴史|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/kumanokusubi/|access-date=2021-09-28|website=A History of Japan - 日本歴史|language=en-GB}}</ref><ref>{{cite web | url=https://archive.today/20230320012745/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9369 | title=Kumanokusubi | 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム }}</ref>
Some scholars have identified this ''[[kami]]'' as the ''saijin'' at the shrine [[Kumano Jinja]] in Shimane Prefecture.<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2021a">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9369 |archive-url= https://web.archive.org/web/20230214183150/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9369|access-date=2021-09-28 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム | archive-date=2023-02-14 |language=ja}}</ref>
== Name == He goes by other names like '''Kumano no oshihomi no mikoto''', '''Kumano no oshikuma no mikoto''', '''Kumano no oshisumi no mikoto''', and '''Kumano no osumi no mikoto.'''<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2021a" />
== Summary == He was born out of a kami making competition between Amaterasu and [[Susanoo]].
In many versions, Susanoo took Amaterasu's beads and crushed them within his mouth, which created five male kami.<ref>{{cite book |author=Seigo Takahashi |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=H18tAAAAYAAJ&dq=Amaterasu+and+Susanoo+oath&pg=PA66 |title=A Study of the Origin of the Japanese State |publisher=W. D. Gray |year=1917}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Amenooshihomimi • A History of Japan - 日本歴史 |url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/amenooshihomimi/ |access-date=2020-09-28 |website=A History of Japan - 日本歴史 |language=en-GB}}</ref> The first one to be born was [[Ame-no-oshihomimi|Amenooshihomimi]], second was [[Ame-no-hohi]], third was [[Amatsuhikone]], fourth was [[Ikutsuhikone]], and [[Kumanokusubi]] was the fifth.<ref>{{Cite book |last1=Havens |first1=Norman |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a_YQAQAAIAAJ&q=Amenooshihomimi |title=An Encyclopedia of Shinto (Shinto Jiten): Kami |last2=Inoue |first2=Nobutaka |date=2006 |publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics Kokugakuin University |isbn=978-4-905853-08-4 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Shinto Portal - IJCC, Kokugakuin University |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=33}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |year=1990 |title=The Journal of the Association of Teachers of Japanese |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Q5cqAQAAIAAJ&q=Amatsuhikone}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Kumanokusubi |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=99 |access-date=2020-10-07 |website=eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp}}</ref>
== Related to Kumano ==
The deity's name, Kusubi (Kusuhi), is thought to mean "strange spirit" (mysterious divine spirit) or "strange fire. The current deity of Kumano-taisha is "Kumano-taishin Kushimikino- no-mikoto," but there is a theory that the original deity was Kumanokusubi.
Kumano-taishin Kushimikino- no-mikoto," but there is a theory that the original deity was Kumanokusubi. There is a theory that the deity of [[Kumano Nachi Taisha]], Kumanokusubi, is [[Izanami|Izamiami]], but this is also believed to be a reference to Kumanokusubi.<ref>{{Cite book|title="Nihon no kami yomi kakkai jiten"|publisher=Kashiwa Shobo|others=Kawaguchi Kenji (ed.)|year=1999|isbn=978-4-7601-1824-3|language=ja}}</ref>}

== Name == He goes by other names like '''Kumano no oshihomi no mikoto''', '''Kumano no oshikuma no mikoto''', '''Kumano no oshisumi no mikoto''', and '''Kumano no osumi no mikoto.'''<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2021a" />
== Summary == He was born out of a kami-making competition between Amaterasu and [[Susanoo]].
In many versions, Susanoo took Amaterasu's beads and crushed them within his mouth, which created five male kami.<ref>{{cite book |author=Seigo Takahashi |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=H18tAAAAYAAJ&dq=Amaterasu+and+Susanoo+oath&pg=PA66 |title=A Study of the Origin of the Japanese State |publisher=W. D. Gray |year=1917}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Amenooshihomimi • A History of Japan - 日本歴史 |url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/amenooshihomimi/ |access-date=2020-09-28 |website=A History of Japan - 日本歴史 |language=en-GB}}</ref> The first one to be born was [[Ame-no-oshihomimi|Amenooshihomimi]], second was [[Ame-no-hohi]], third was [[Amatsuhikone]], fourth was [[Ikutsuhikone]], and [[Kumanokusubi]] was the fifth.<ref>{{Cite book |last1=Havens |first1=Norman |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a_YQAQAAIAAJ&q=Amenooshihomimi |title=An Encyclopedia of Shinto (Shinto Jiten): Kami |last2=Inoue |first2=Nobutaka |date=2006 |publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics Kokugakuin University |isbn=978-4-905853-08-4 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Shinto Portal - IJCC, Kokugakuin University |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=33}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |year=1990 |title=The Journal of the Association of Teachers of Japanese |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Q5cqAQAAIAAJ&q=Amatsuhikone}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Kumanokusubi |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=99 |access-date=2020-10-07 |website=eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp}}</ref>
== Related to Kumano ==
The deity's name, Kusubi (Kusuhi), is thought to mean "strange spirit" (mysterious divine spirit) or "strange fire. The current deity of Kumano-taisha is "Kumano-taishin Kushimikino- no-mikoto," but there is a theory that the original deity was Kumanokusubi.
Kumano-taishin Kushimikino- no-mikoto," but there is a theory that the original deity was Kumanokusubi. There is a theory that the deity of [[Kumano Nachi Taisha]], Kumanokusubi, is [[Izanami|Izamiami]], but this is also believed to be a reference to Kumanokusubi.<ref>{{Cite book|title="Nihon no kami yomi kakkai jiten"|publisher=Kashiwa Shobo|others=Kawaguchi Kenji (ed.)|year=1999|isbn=978-4-7601-1824-3|language=ja}}</ref>}

There is a theory that the deity of [[Kumano Nachi Taisha]], Kumanokusubi, is [[Izanami|Izamiami]], but this is also believed to be a reference to Kumanokusubi.<ref>{{Cite book|title="Nihon no kami yomi kakkai jiten"|publisher=Kashiwa Shobo|others=Kawaguchi Kenji (ed.)|year=1999|isbn=978-4-7601-1824-3|language=ja}}</ref>}

{{Short description|God in Japanese mythology}} {{refimprove|date=January 2022}} {{Infobox deity | type = Shinto | name = Kumanokusubi | siblings = [[Ame-no-hohi]] (brother) [[Ame-no-oshihomimi]] (brother) [[Ikutsuhikone]] (brother) [[Amatsuhikone]] (brother) | mother = [[Amaterasu]] }}[[File:Children born from the Challenge of Amaterasu and Susanoo-en.svg|thumb|240x240px|Oath between Amaterasu and Susanowo (based on the Kojiki)]]
'''Kumanokusubi''' (熊野久须毘命,熊野櫲樟日命, Wonder Worker of Bear Moors)<ref name="a-history-of-japan-日本歴史-2021a" /> is a [[Kami|God]] in [[Japanese mythology]]. He is the fifth son of [[Amaterasu]].<ref name="a-history-of-japan-日本歴史-2021a">{{Cite web|title=Kumanokusubi • A History of Japan - 日本歴史|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/kumanokusubi/|access-date=2021-09-28|website=A History of Japan - 日本歴史|language=en-GB}}</ref><ref>{{cite web | url=https://archive.today/20230320012745/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9369 | title=Kumanokusubi | 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム }}</ref>
Some scholars have identified this ''[[kami]]'' as the ''saijin'' at the shrine [[Kumano Jinja]] in Shimane Prefecture.<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2021a">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9369 |archive-url= https://web.archive.org/web/20230214183150/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9369|access-date=2021-09-28 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム | archive-date=2023-02-14 |language=ja}}</ref>
== Name == He goes by other names like '''Kumano no oshihomi no mikoto''', '''Kumano no oshikuma no mikoto''', '''Kumano no oshisumi no mikoto''', and '''Kumano no osumi no mikoto.'''<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2021a" />
== Summary == He was born out of a kami-making competition between Amaterasu and [[Susanoo]].
In many versions, Susanoo took Amaterasu's beads and crushed them within his mouth, which created five male kami.<ref>{{cite book |author=Seigo Takahashi |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=H18tAAAAYAAJ&dq=Amaterasu+and+Susanoo+oath&pg=PA66 |title=A Study of the Origin of the Japanese State |publisher=W. D. Gray |year=1917}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Amenooshihomimi • A History of Japan - 日本歴史 |url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/amenooshihomimi/ |access-date=2020-09-28 |website=A History of Japan - 日本歴史 |language=en-GB}}</ref> The first one to be born was [[Ame-no-oshihomimi|Amenooshihomimi]], second was [[Ame-no-hohi]], third was [[Amatsuhikone]], fourth was [[Ikutsuhikone]], and [[Kumanokusubi]] was the fifth.<ref>{{Cite book |last1=Havens |first1=Norman |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a_YQAQAAIAAJ&q=Amenooshihomimi |title=An Encyclopedia of Shinto (Shinto Jiten): Kami |last2=Inoue |first2=Nobutaka |date=2006 |publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics Kokugakuin University |isbn=978-4-905853-08-4 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Shinto Portal - IJCC, Kokugakuin University |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=33}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |year=1990 |title=The Journal of the Association of Teachers of Japanese |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Q5cqAQAAIAAJ&q=Amatsuhikone}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Kumanokusubi |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=99 |access-date=2020-10-07 |website=eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp}}</ref>
== Related to Kumano ==
The deity's name, Kusubi (Kusuhi), is thought to mean "strange spirit" (mysterious divine spirit) or "strange fire. The current deity of Kumano-taisha is "Kumano-taishin Kushimikino- no-mikoto," but there is a theory that the original deity was Kumanokusubi.
Kumano-taishin Kushimikino- no-mikoto," but there is a theory that the original deity was Kumanokusubi. There is a theory that the deity of [[Kumano Nachi Taisha]], Kumanokusubi, is [[Izanami|Izamiami]], but this is also believed to be a reference to Kumanokusubi.<ref>{{Cite book|title="Nihon no kami yomi kakkai jiten"|publisher=Kashiwa Shobo|others=Kawaguchi Kenji (ed.)|year=1999|isbn=978-4-7601-1824-3|language=ja}}</ref>}

Gray |year=1917}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Amenooshihomimi • A History of Japan - 日本歴史 |url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/amenooshihomimi/ |access-date=2020-09-28 |website=A History of Japan - 日本歴史 |language=en-GB}}</ref> The first one to be born was [[Ame-no-oshihomimi|Amenooshihomimi]], second was [[Ame-no-hohi]], third was [[Amatsuhikone]], fourth was [[Ikutsuhikone]], and [[Kumanokusubi]] was the fifth.<ref>{{Cite book |last1=Havens |first1=Norman |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a_YQAQAAIAAJ&q=Amenooshihomimi |title=An Encyclopedia of Shinto (Shinto Jiten): Kami |last2=Inoue |first2=Nobutaka |date=2006 |publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics Kokugakuin University |isbn=978-4-905853-08-4 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Shinto Portal - IJCC, Kokugakuin University |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=33}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |year=1990 |title=The Journal of the Association of Teachers of Japanese |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Q5cqAQAAIAAJ&q=Amatsuhikone}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Kumanokusubi |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=99 |access-date=2020-10-07 |website=eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp}}</ref>
== Related to Kumano ==
The deity's name, Kusubi (Kusuhi), is thought to mean "strange spirit" (mysterious divine spirit) or "strange fire. The current deity of Kumano-taisha is "Kumano-taishin Kushimikino- no-mikoto," but there is a theory that the original deity was Kumanokusubi.
Kumano-taishin Kushimikino- no-mikoto," but there is a theory that the original deity was Kumanokusubi. There is a theory that the deity of [[Kumano Nachi Taisha]], Kumanokusubi, is [[Izanami|Izamiami]], but this is also believed to be a reference to Kumanokusubi.<ref>{{Cite book|title="Nihon no kami yomi kakkai jiten"|publisher=Kashiwa Shobo|others=Kawaguchi Kenji (ed.)|year=1999|isbn=978-4-7601-1824-3|language=ja}}</ref>}

He is the fifth son of [[Amaterasu]].<ref name="a-history-of-japan-日本歴史-2021a">{{Cite web|title=Kumanokusubi • A History of Japan - 日本歴史|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/kumanokusubi/|access-date=2021-09-28|website=A History of Japan - 日本歴史|language=en-GB}}</ref><ref>{{cite web | url=https://archive.today/20230320012745/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9369 | title=Kumanokusubi | 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム }}</ref>
Some scholars have identified this ''[[kami]]'' as the ''saijin'' at the shrine [[Kumano Jinja]] in Shimane Prefecture.<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2021a">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9369 |archive-url= https://web.archive.org/web/20230214183150/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9369|access-date=2021-09-28 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム | archive-date=2023-02-14 |language=ja}}</ref>

D. Gray |year=1917}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Amenooshihomimi • A History of Japan - 日本歴史 |url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/amenooshihomimi/ |access-date=2020-09-28 |website=A History of Japan - 日本歴史 |language=en-GB}}</ref> The first one to be born was [[Ame-no-oshihomimi|Amenooshihomimi]], second was [[Ame-no-hohi]], third was [[Amatsuhikone]], fourth was [[Ikutsuhikone]], and [[Kumanokusubi]] was the fifth.<ref>{{Cite book |last1=Havens |first1=Norman |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a_YQAQAAIAAJ&q=Amenooshihomimi |title=An Encyclopedia of Shinto (Shinto Jiten): Kami |last2=Inoue |first2=Nobutaka |date=2006 |publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics Kokugakuin University |isbn=978-4-905853-08-4 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Shinto Portal - IJCC, Kokugakuin University |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=33}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |year=1990 |title=The Journal of the Association of Teachers of Japanese |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Q5cqAQAAIAAJ&q=Amatsuhikone}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Kumanokusubi |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=99 |access-date=2020-10-07 |website=eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp}}</ref>

Kumano-taishin Kushimikino- no-mikoto," but there is a theory that the original deity was Kumanokusubi. There is a theory that the deity of [[Kumano Nachi Taisha]], Kumanokusubi, is [[Izanami|Izamiami]], but this is also believed to be a reference to Kumanokusubi.<ref>{{Cite book|title="Nihon no kami yomi kakkai jiten"|publisher=Kashiwa Shobo|others=Kawaguchi Kenji (ed.)|year=1999|isbn=978-4-7601-1824-3|language=ja}}</ref>}

'''Kumanokusubi''' (熊野久须毘命,熊野櫲樟日命, Wonder Worker of Bear Moors)<ref name="a-history-of-japan-日本歴史-2021a" /> is a [[Kami|God]] in [[Japanese mythology]]. He is the fifth son of [[Amaterasu]].<ref name="a-history-of-japan-日本歴史-2021a">{{Cite web|title=Kumanokusubi • A History of Japan - 日本歴史|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/kumanokusubi/|access-date=2021-09-28|website=A History of Japan - 日本歴史|language=en-GB}}</ref><ref>{{cite web | url=https://archive.today/20230320012745/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9369 | title=Kumanokusubi | 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム }}</ref>
Some scholars have identified this ''[[kami]]'' as the ''saijin'' at the shrine [[Kumano Jinja]] in Shimane Prefecture.<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2021a">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9369 |archive-url= https://web.archive.org/web/20230214183150/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9369|access-date=2021-09-28 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム | archive-date=2023-02-14 |language=ja}}</ref>
== Name == He goes by other names like '''Kumano no oshihomi no mikoto''', '''Kumano no oshikuma no mikoto''', '''Kumano no oshisumi no mikoto''', and '''Kumano no osumi no mikoto.'''<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2021a" />
== Summary == He was born out of a kami-making competition between Amaterasu and [[Susanoo]].
In many versions, Susanoo took Amaterasu's beads and crushed them within his mouth, which created five male kami.<ref>{{cite book |author=Seigo Takahashi |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=H18tAAAAYAAJ&dq=Amaterasu+and+Susanoo+oath&pg=PA66 |title=A Study of the Origin of the Japanese State |publisher=W. D. Gray |year=1917}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Amenooshihomimi • A History of Japan - 日本歴史 |url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/amenooshihomimi/ |access-date=2020-09-28 |website=A History of Japan - 日本歴史 |language=en-GB}}</ref> The first one to be born was [[Ame-no-oshihomimi|Amenooshihomimi]], second was [[Ame-no-hohi]], third was [[Amatsuhikone]], fourth was [[Ikutsuhikone]], and [[Kumanokusubi]] was the fifth.<ref>{{Cite book |last1=Havens |first1=Norman |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a_YQAQAAIAAJ&q=Amenooshihomimi |title=An Encyclopedia of Shinto (Shinto Jiten): Kami |last2=Inoue |first2=Nobutaka |date=2006 |publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics Kokugakuin University |isbn=978-4-905853-08-4 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Shinto Portal - IJCC, Kokugakuin University |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=33}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |year=1990 |title=The Journal of the Association of Teachers of Japanese |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Q5cqAQAAIAAJ&q=Amatsuhikone}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Kumanokusubi |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=99 |access-date=2020-10-07 |website=eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp}}</ref>
== Related to Kumano ==
The deity's name, Kusubi (Kusuhi), is thought to mean "strange spirit" (mysterious divine spirit) or "strange fire. The current deity of Kumano-taisha is "Kumano-taishin Kushimikino- no-mikoto," but there is a theory that the original deity was Kumanokusubi.
Kumano-taishin Kushimikino- no-mikoto," but there is a theory that the original deity was Kumanokusubi. There is a theory that the deity of [[Kumano Nachi Taisha]], Kumanokusubi, is [[Izanami|Izamiami]], but this is also believed to be a reference to Kumanokusubi.<ref>{{Cite book|title="Nihon no kami yomi kakkai jiten"|publisher=Kashiwa Shobo|others=Kawaguchi Kenji (ed.)|year=1999|isbn=978-4-7601-1824-3|language=ja}}</ref>}

The deity's name, Kusubi (Kusuhi), is thought to mean "strange spirit" (mysterious divine spirit) or "strange fire. The current deity of Kumano-taisha is "Kumano-taishin Kushimikino- no-mikoto," but there is a theory that the original deity was Kumanokusubi.
Kumano-taishin Kushimikino- no-mikoto," but there is a theory that the original deity was Kumanokusubi. There is a theory that the deity of [[Kumano Nachi Taisha]], Kumanokusubi, is [[Izanami|Izamiami]], but this is also believed to be a reference to Kumanokusubi.<ref>{{Cite book|title="Nihon no kami yomi kakkai jiten"|publisher=Kashiwa Shobo|others=Kawaguchi Kenji (ed.)|year=1999|isbn=978-4-7601-1824-3|language=ja}}</ref>}

{{Short description|God in Japanese mythology}} {{refimprove|date=January 2022}} {{Infobox deity | type = Shinto | name = Kumanokusubi | siblings = [[Ame-no-hohi]] (brother) [[Ame-no-oshihomimi]] (brother) [[Ikutsuhikone]] (brother) [[Amatsuhikone]] (brother) | mother = [[Amaterasu]] }}[[File:Children born from the Challenge of Amaterasu and Susanoo-en.svg|thumb|240x240px|Oath between Amaterasu and Susanowo (based on the Kojiki)]]
'''Kumanokusubi''' (熊野久须毘命,熊野櫲樟日命, Wonder Worker of Bear Moors)<ref name="a-history-of-japan-日本歴史-2021a" /> is a [[Kami|God]] in [[Japanese mythology]]. He is the fifth son of [[Amaterasu]].<ref name="a-history-of-japan-日本歴史-2021a">{{Cite web|title=Kumanokusubi • A History of Japan - 日本歴史|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/kumanokusubi/|access-date=2021-09-28|website=A History of Japan - 日本歴史|language=en-GB}}</ref><ref>{{cite web | url=https://archive.today/20230320012745/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9369 | title=Kumanokusubi | 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム }}</ref>
Some scholars have identified this ''[[kami]]'' as the ''saijin'' at the shrine [[Kumano Jinja]] in Shimane Prefecture.<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2021a">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9369 |archive-url= https://web.archive.org/web/20230214183150/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9369|access-date=2021-09-28 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム | archive-date=2023-02-14 |language=ja}}</ref>
== Name == He goes by other names like '''Kumano no oshihomi no mikoto''', '''Kumano no oshikuma no mikoto''', '''Kumano no oshisumi no mikoto''', and '''Kumano no osumi no mikoto.'''<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2021a" />
== Summary == He was born out of a kami-making competition between Amaterasu and [[Susanoo]].
In many versions, Susanoo took Amaterasu's beads and crushed them within his mouth, which created five male kami.<ref>{{cite book |author=Seigo Takahashi |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=H18tAAAAYAAJ&dq=Amaterasu+and+Susanoo+oath&pg=PA66 |title=A Study of the Origin of the Japanese State |publisher=W. D. Gray |year=1917}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Amenooshihomimi • A History of Japan - 日本歴史 |url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/amenooshihomimi/ |access-date=2020-09-28 |website=A History of Japan - 日本歴史 |language=en-GB}}</ref> The first one to be born was [[Ame-no-oshihomimi|Amenooshihomimi]], second was [[Ame-no-hohi]], third was [[Amatsuhikone]], fourth was [[Ikutsuhikone]], and [[Kumanokusubi]] was the fifth.<ref>{{Cite book |last1=Havens |first1=Norman |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a_YQAQAAIAAJ&q=Amenooshihomimi |title=An Encyclopedia of Shinto (Shinto Jiten): Kami |last2=Inoue |first2=Nobutaka |date=2006 |publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics Kokugakuin University |isbn=978-4-905853-08-4 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Shinto Portal - IJCC, Kokugakuin University |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=33}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |year=1990 |title=The Journal of the Association of Teachers of Japanese |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Q5cqAQAAIAAJ&q=Amatsuhikone}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Kumanokusubi |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=99 |access-date=2020-10-07 |website=eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp}}</ref>
== Related to Kumano ==
The deity's name, Kusubi (Kusuhi), is thought to mean "strange spirit" (mysterious divine spirit) or "strange fire. The current deity of Kumano-taisha is "Kumano-taishin Kushimikino- no-mikoto," but there is a theory that the original deity was Kumanokusubi.
Kumano-taishin Kushimikino- no-mikoto," but there is a theory that the original deity was Kumanokusubi. There is a theory that the deity of [[Kumano Nachi Taisha]], Kumanokusubi, is [[Izanami|Izamiami]], but this is also believed to be a reference to Kumanokusubi.<ref>{{Cite book|title="Nihon no kami yomi kakkai jiten"|publisher=Kashiwa Shobo|others=Kawaguchi Kenji (ed.)|year=1999|isbn=978-4-7601-1824-3|language=ja}}</ref>}

In many versions, Susanoo took Amaterasu's beads and crushed them within his mouth, which created five male kami.<ref>{{cite book |author=Seigo Takahashi |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=H18tAAAAYAAJ&dq=Amaterasu+and+Susanoo+oath&pg=PA66 |title=A Study of the Origin of the Japanese State |publisher=W. D. Gray |year=1917}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Amenooshihomimi • A History of Japan - 日本歴史 |url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/amenooshihomimi/ |access-date=2020-09-28 |website=A History of Japan - 日本歴史 |language=en-GB}}</ref> The first one to be born was [[Ame-no-oshihomimi|Amenooshihomimi]], second was [[Ame-no-hohi]], third was [[Amatsuhikone]], fourth was [[Ikutsuhikone]], and [[Kumanokusubi]] was the fifth.<ref>{{Cite book |last1=Havens |first1=Norman |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a_YQAQAAIAAJ&q=Amenooshihomimi |title=An Encyclopedia of Shinto (Shinto Jiten): Kami |last2=Inoue |first2=Nobutaka |date=2006 |publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics Kokugakuin University |isbn=978-4-905853-08-4 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Shinto Portal - IJCC, Kokugakuin University |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=33}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |year=1990 |title=The Journal of the Association of Teachers of Japanese |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Q5cqAQAAIAAJ&q=Amatsuhikone}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Kumanokusubi |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=99 |access-date=2020-10-07 |website=eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp}}</ref>
== Related to Kumano ==
The deity's name, Kusubi (Kusuhi), is thought to mean "strange spirit" (mysterious divine spirit) or "strange fire. The current deity of Kumano-taisha is "Kumano-taishin Kushimikino- no-mikoto," but there is a theory that the original deity was Kumanokusubi.
Kumano-taishin Kushimikino- no-mikoto," but there is a theory that the original deity was Kumanokusubi. There is a theory that the deity of [[Kumano Nachi Taisha]], Kumanokusubi, is [[Izanami|Izamiami]], but this is also believed to be a reference to Kumanokusubi.<ref>{{Cite book|title="Nihon no kami yomi kakkai jiten"|publisher=Kashiwa Shobo|others=Kawaguchi Kenji (ed.)|year=1999|isbn=978-4-7601-1824-3|language=ja}}</ref>}

'''Kumanokusubi''' (熊野久须毘命,熊野櫲樟日命, Wonder Worker of Bear Moors)<ref name="a-history-of-japan-日本歴史-2021a" /> is a [[Kami|God]] in [[Japanese mythology]]. He is the fifth son of [[Amaterasu]].<ref name="a-history-of-japan-日本歴史-2021a">{{Cite web|title=Kumanokusubi • A History of Japan - 日本歴史|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/kumanokusubi/|access-date=2021-09-28|website=A History of Japan - 日本歴史|language=en-GB}}</ref><ref>{{cite web | url=https://archive.today/20230320012745/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9369 | title=Kumanokusubi | 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム }}</ref>
Some scholars have identified this ''[[kami]]'' as the ''saijin'' at the shrine [[Kumano Jinja]] in Shimane Prefecture.<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2021a">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9369 |archive-url= https://web.archive.org/web/20230214183150/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9369|access-date=2021-09-28 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム | archive-date=2023-02-14 |language=ja}}</ref>
== Name == He goes by other names like '''Kumano no oshihomi no mikoto''', '''Kumano no oshikuma no mikoto''', '''Kumano no oshisumi no mikoto''', and '''Kumano no osumi no mikoto.'''<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2021a" />
== Summary == He was born out of a kami-making competition between Amaterasu and [[Susanoo]].
In many versions, Susanoo took Amaterasu's beads and crushed them within his mouth, which created five male kami.<ref>{{cite book |author=Seigo Takahashi |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=H18tAAAAYAAJ&dq=Amaterasu+and+Susanoo+oath&pg=PA66 |title=A Study of the Origin of the Japanese State |publisher=W. D. Gray |year=1917}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Amenooshihomimi • A History of Japan - 日本歴史 |url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/amenooshihomimi/ |access-date=2020-09-28 |website=A History of Japan - 日本歴史 |language=en-GB}}</ref> The first one to be born was [[Ame-no-oshihomimi|Amenooshihomimi]], second was [[Ame-no-hohi]], third was [[Amatsuhikone]], fourth was [[Ikutsuhikone]], and [[Kumanokusubi]] was the fifth.<ref>{{Cite book |last1=Havens |first1=Norman |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a_YQAQAAIAAJ&q=Amenooshihomimi |title=An Encyclopedia of Shinto (Shinto Jiten): Kami |last2=Inoue |first2=Nobutaka |date=2006 |publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics Kokugakuin University |isbn=978-4-905853-08-4 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Shinto Portal - IJCC, Kokugakuin University |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=33}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |year=1990 |title=The Journal of the Association of Teachers of Japanese |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Q5cqAQAAIAAJ&q=Amatsuhikone}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Kumanokusubi |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=99 |access-date=2020-10-07 |website=eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp}}</ref>
== Related to Kumano ==
The deity's name, Kusubi (Kusuhi), is thought to mean "strange spirit" (mysterious divine spirit) or "strange fire. The current deity of Kumano-taisha is "Kumano-taishin Kushimikino- no-mikoto," but there is a theory that the original deity was Kumanokusubi.
Kumano-taishin Kushimikino- no-mikoto," but there is a theory that the original deity was Kumanokusubi. There is a theory that the deity of [[Kumano Nachi Taisha]], Kumanokusubi, is [[Izanami|Izamiami]], but this is also believed to be a reference to Kumanokusubi.<ref>{{Cite book|title="Nihon no kami yomi kakkai jiten"|publisher=Kashiwa Shobo|others=Kawaguchi Kenji (ed.)|year=1999|isbn=978-4-7601-1824-3|language=ja}}</ref>}

There is a theory that the deity of [[Kumano Nachi Taisha]], Kumanokusubi, is [[Izanami|Izamiami]], but this is also believed to be a reference to Kumanokusubi.<ref>{{Cite book|title="Nihon no kami yomi kakkai jiten"|publisher=Kashiwa Shobo|others=Kawaguchi Kenji (ed.)|year=1999|isbn=978-4-7601-1824-3|language=ja}}</ref>}

{{Short description|God in Japanese mythology}} {{refimprove|date=January 2022}} {{Infobox deity | type = Shinto | name = Kumanokusubi | siblings = [[Ame-no-hohi]] (brother) [[Ame-no-oshihomimi]] (brother) [[Ikutsuhikone]] (brother) [[Amatsuhikone]] (brother) | mother = [[Amaterasu]] }}[[File:Children born from the Challenge of Amaterasu and Susanoo-en.svg|thumb|240x240px|Oath between Amaterasu and Susanowo (based on the Kojiki)]]
'''Kumanokusubi''' (熊野久须毘命,熊野櫲樟日命, Wonder Worker of Bear Moors)<ref name="a-history-of-japan-日本歴史-2021a" /> is a [[Kami|God]] in [[Japanese mythology]]. He is the fifth son of [[Amaterasu]].<ref name="a-history-of-japan-日本歴史-2021a">{{Cite web|title=Kumanokusubi • A History of Japan - 日本歴史|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/kumanokusubi/|access-date=2021-09-28|website=A History of Japan - 日本歴史|language=en-GB}}</ref><ref>{{cite web | url=https://archive.today/20230320012745/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9369 | title=Kumanokusubi | 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム }}</ref>
Some scholars have identified this ''[[kami]]'' as the ''saijin'' at the shrine [[Kumano Jinja]] in Shimane Prefecture.<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2021a">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9369 |archive-url= https://web.archive.org/web/20230214183150/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9369|access-date=2021-09-28 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム | archive-date=2023-02-14 |language=ja}}</ref>
== Name == He goes by other names like '''Kumano no oshihomi no mikoto''', '''Kumano no oshikuma no mikoto''', '''Kumano no oshisumi no mikoto''', and '''Kumano no osumi no mikoto.'''<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2021a" />
== Summary == He was born out of a kami-making competition between Amaterasu and [[Susanoo]].
In many versions, Susanoo took Amaterasu's beads and crushed them within his mouth, which created five male kami.<ref>{{cite book |author=Seigo Takahashi |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=H18tAAAAYAAJ&dq=Amaterasu+and+Susanoo+oath&pg=PA66 |title=A Study of the Origin of the Japanese State |publisher=W. D. Gray |year=1917}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Amenooshihomimi • A History of Japan - 日本歴史 |url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/amenooshihomimi/ |access-date=2020-09-28 |website=A History of Japan - 日本歴史 |language=en-GB}}</ref> The first one to be born was [[Ame-no-oshihomimi|Amenooshihomimi]], second was [[Ame-no-hohi]], third was [[Amatsuhikone]], fourth was [[Ikutsuhikone]], and [[Kumanokusubi]] was the fifth.<ref>{{Cite book |last1=Havens |first1=Norman |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a_YQAQAAIAAJ&q=Amenooshihomimi |title=An Encyclopedia of Shinto (Shinto Jiten): Kami |last2=Inoue |first2=Nobutaka |date=2006 |publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics Kokugakuin University |isbn=978-4-905853-08-4 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Shinto Portal - IJCC, Kokugakuin University |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=33}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |year=1990 |title=The Journal of the Association of Teachers of Japanese |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Q5cqAQAAIAAJ&q=Amatsuhikone}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Kumanokusubi |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=99 |access-date=2020-10-07 |website=eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp}}</ref>
== Related to Kumano ==
The deity's name, Kusubi (Kusuhi), is thought to mean "strange spirit" (mysterious divine spirit) or "strange fire. The current deity of Kumano-taisha is "Kumano-taishin Kushimikino- no-mikoto," but there is a theory that the original deity was Kumanokusubi.
Kumano-taishin Kushimikino- no-mikoto," but there is a theory that the original deity was Kumanokusubi. There is a theory that the deity of [[Kumano Nachi Taisha]], Kumanokusubi, is [[Izanami|Izamiami]], but this is also believed to be a reference to Kumanokusubi.<ref>{{Cite book|title="Nihon no kami yomi kakkai jiten"|publisher=Kashiwa Shobo|others=Kawaguchi Kenji (ed.)|year=1999|isbn=978-4-7601-1824-3|language=ja}}</ref>}

Some scholars have identified this ''[[kami]]'' as the ''saijin'' at the shrine [[Kumano Jinja]] in Shimane Prefecture.<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2021a">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9369 |archive-url= https://web.archive.org/web/20230214183150/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9369|access-date=2021-09-28 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム | archive-date=2023-02-14 |language=ja}}</ref>
== Name == He goes by other names like '''Kumano no oshihomi no mikoto''', '''Kumano no oshikuma no mikoto''', '''Kumano no oshisumi no mikoto''', and '''Kumano no osumi no mikoto.'''<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2021a" />
== Summary == He was born out of a kami-making competition between Amaterasu and [[Susanoo]].
In many versions, Susanoo took Amaterasu's beads and crushed them within his mouth, which created five male kami.<ref>{{cite book |author=Seigo Takahashi |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=H18tAAAAYAAJ&dq=Amaterasu+and+Susanoo+oath&pg=PA66 |title=A Study of the Origin of the Japanese State |publisher=W. D. Gray |year=1917}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Amenooshihomimi • A History of Japan - 日本歴史 |url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/amenooshihomimi/ |access-date=2020-09-28 |website=A History of Japan - 日本歴史 |language=en-GB}}</ref> The first one to be born was [[Ame-no-oshihomimi|Amenooshihomimi]], second was [[Ame-no-hohi]], third was [[Amatsuhikone]], fourth was [[Ikutsuhikone]], and [[Kumanokusubi]] was the fifth.<ref>{{Cite book |last1=Havens |first1=Norman |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a_YQAQAAIAAJ&q=Amenooshihomimi |title=An Encyclopedia of Shinto (Shinto Jiten): Kami |last2=Inoue |first2=Nobutaka |date=2006 |publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics Kokugakuin University |isbn=978-4-905853-08-4 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Shinto Portal - IJCC, Kokugakuin University |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=33}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |year=1990 |title=The Journal of the Association of Teachers of Japanese |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Q5cqAQAAIAAJ&q=Amatsuhikone}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Kumanokusubi |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=99 |access-date=2020-10-07 |website=eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp}}</ref>
== Related to Kumano ==
The deity's name, Kusubi (Kusuhi), is thought to mean "strange spirit" (mysterious divine spirit) or "strange fire. The current deity of Kumano-taisha is "Kumano-taishin Kushimikino- no-mikoto," but there is a theory that the original deity was Kumanokusubi.
Kumano-taishin Kushimikino- no-mikoto," but there is a theory that the original deity was Kumanokusubi. There is a theory that the deity of [[Kumano Nachi Taisha]], Kumanokusubi, is [[Izanami|Izamiami]], but this is also believed to be a reference to Kumanokusubi.<ref>{{Cite book|title="Nihon no kami yomi kakkai jiten"|publisher=Kashiwa Shobo|others=Kawaguchi Kenji (ed.)|year=1999|isbn=978-4-7601-1824-3|language=ja}}</ref>}

{{Short description|God in Japanese mythology}} {{refimprove|date=January 2022}} {{Infobox deity | type = Shinto | name = Kumanokusubi | siblings = [[Ame-no-hohi]] (brother) [[Ame-no-oshihomimi]] (brother) [[Ikutsuhikone]] (brother) [[Amatsuhikone]] (brother) | mother = [[Amaterasu]] }}[[File:Children born from the Challenge of Amaterasu and Susanoo-en.svg|thumb|240x240px|Oath between Amaterasu and Susanowo (based on the Kojiki)]]
'''Kumanokusubi''' (熊野久须毘命,熊野櫲樟日命, Wonder Worker of Bear Moors)<ref name="a-history-of-japan-日本歴史-2021a" /> is a [[Kami|God]] in [[Japanese mythology]]. He is the fifth son of [[Amaterasu]].<ref name="a-history-of-japan-日本歴史-2021a">{{Cite web|title=Kumanokusubi • A History of Japan - 日本歴史|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/kumanokusubi/|access-date=2021-09-28|website=A History of Japan - 日本歴史|language=en-GB}}</ref><ref>{{cite web | url=https://archive.today/20230320012745/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9369 | title=Kumanokusubi | 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム }}</ref>
Some scholars have identified this ''[[kami]]'' as the ''saijin'' at the shrine [[Kumano Jinja]] in Shimane Prefecture.<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2021a">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9369 |archive-url= https://web.archive.org/web/20230214183150/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9369|access-date=2021-09-28 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム | archive-date=2023-02-14 |language=ja}}</ref>
== Name == He goes by other names like '''Kumano no oshihomi no mikoto''', '''Kumano no oshikuma no mikoto''', '''Kumano no oshisumi no mikoto''', and '''Kumano no osumi no mikoto.'''<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2021a" />
== Summary == He was born out of a kami-making competition between Amaterasu and [[Susanoo]].
In many versions, Susanoo took Amaterasu's beads and crushed them within his mouth, which created five male kami.<ref>{{cite book |author=Seigo Takahashi |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=H18tAAAAYAAJ&dq=Amaterasu+and+Susanoo+oath&pg=PA66 |title=A Study of the Origin of the Japanese State |publisher=W. D. Gray |year=1917}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Amenooshihomimi • A History of Japan - 日本歴史 |url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/amenooshihomimi/ |access-date=2020-09-28 |website=A History of Japan - 日本歴史 |language=en-GB}}</ref> The first one to be born was [[Ame-no-oshihomimi|Amenooshihomimi]], second was [[Ame-no-hohi]], third was [[Amatsuhikone]], fourth was [[Ikutsuhikone]], and [[Kumanokusubi]] was the fifth.<ref>{{Cite book |last1=Havens |first1=Norman |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a_YQAQAAIAAJ&q=Amenooshihomimi |title=An Encyclopedia of Shinto (Shinto Jiten): Kami |last2=Inoue |first2=Nobutaka |date=2006 |publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics Kokugakuin University |isbn=978-4-905853-08-4 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Shinto Portal - IJCC, Kokugakuin University |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=33}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |year=1990 |title=The Journal of the Association of Teachers of Japanese |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Q5cqAQAAIAAJ&q=Amatsuhikone}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Kumanokusubi |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=99 |access-date=2020-10-07 |website=eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp}}</ref>
== Related to Kumano ==
The deity's name, Kusubi (Kusuhi), is thought to mean "strange spirit" (mysterious divine spirit) or "strange fire. The current deity of Kumano-taisha is "Kumano-taishin Kushimikino- no-mikoto," but there is a theory that the original deity was Kumanokusubi.
Kumano-taishin Kushimikino- no-mikoto," but there is a theory that the original deity was Kumanokusubi. There is a theory that the deity of [[Kumano Nachi Taisha]], Kumanokusubi, is [[Izanami|Izamiami]], but this is also believed to be a reference to Kumanokusubi.<ref>{{Cite book|title="Nihon no kami yomi kakkai jiten"|publisher=Kashiwa Shobo|others=Kawaguchi Kenji (ed.)|year=1999|isbn=978-4-7601-1824-3|language=ja}}</ref>}

'''Kumanokusubi''' (熊野久须毘命,熊野櫲樟日命, Wonder Worker of Bear Moors)<ref name="a-history-of-japan-日本歴史-2021a" /> is a [[Kami|God]] in [[Japanese mythology]]. He is the fifth son of [[Amaterasu]].<ref name="a-history-of-japan-日本歴史-2021a">{{Cite web|title=Kumanokusubi • A History of Japan - 日本歴史|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/kumanokusubi/|access-date=2021-09-28|website=A History of Japan - 日本歴史|language=en-GB}}</ref><ref>{{cite web | url=https://archive.today/20230320012745/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9369 | title=Kumanokusubi | 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム }}</ref>
Some scholars have identified this ''[[kami]]'' as the ''saijin'' at the shrine [[Kumano Jinja]] in Shimane Prefecture.<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2021a">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9369 |archive-url= https://web.archive.org/web/20230214183150/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9369|access-date=2021-09-28 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム | archive-date=2023-02-14 |language=ja}}</ref>

In many versions, Susanoo took Amaterasu's beads and crushed them within his mouth, which created five male kami.<ref>{{cite book |author=Seigo Takahashi |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=H18tAAAAYAAJ&dq=Amaterasu+and+Susanoo+oath&pg=PA66 |title=A Study of the Origin of the Japanese State |publisher=W. D. Gray |year=1917}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Amenooshihomimi • A History of Japan - 日本歴史 |url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/amenooshihomimi/ |access-date=2020-09-28 |website=A History of Japan - 日本歴史 |language=en-GB}}</ref> The first one to be born was [[Ame-no-oshihomimi|Amenooshihomimi]], second was [[Ame-no-hohi]], third was [[Amatsuhikone]], fourth was [[Ikutsuhikone]], and [[Kumanokusubi]] was the fifth.<ref>{{Cite book |last1=Havens |first1=Norman |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a_YQAQAAIAAJ&q=Amenooshihomimi |title=An Encyclopedia of Shinto (Shinto Jiten): Kami |last2=Inoue |first2=Nobutaka |date=2006 |publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics Kokugakuin University |isbn=978-4-905853-08-4 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Shinto Portal - IJCC, Kokugakuin University |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=33}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |year=1990 |title=The Journal of the Association of Teachers of Japanese |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Q5cqAQAAIAAJ&q=Amatsuhikone}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Kumanokusubi |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=99 |access-date=2020-10-07 |website=eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp}}</ref>

The deity's name, Kusubi (Kusuhi), is thought to mean "strange spirit" (mysterious divine spirit) or "strange fire. The current deity of Kumano-taisha is "Kumano-taishin Kushimikino- no-mikoto," but there is a theory that the original deity was Kumanokusubi.
Kumano-taishin Kushimikino- no-mikoto," but there is a theory that the original deity was Kumanokusubi. There is a theory that the deity of [[Kumano Nachi Taisha]], Kumanokusubi, is [[Izanami|Izamiami]], but this is also believed to be a reference to Kumanokusubi.<ref>{{Cite book|title="Nihon no kami yomi kakkai jiten"|publisher=Kashiwa Shobo|others=Kawaguchi Kenji (ed.)|year=1999|isbn=978-4-7601-1824-3|language=ja}}</ref>}

{{Short description|God in Japanese mythology}} {{refimprove|date=January 2022}} {{Infobox deity | type = Shinto | name = Kumanokusubi | siblings = [[Ame-no-hohi]] (brother) [[Ame-no-oshihomimi]] (brother) [[Ikutsuhikone]] (brother) [[Amatsuhikone]] (brother) | mother = [[Amaterasu]] }}[[File:Children born from the Challenge of Amaterasu and Susanoo-en.svg|thumb|240x240px|Oath between Amaterasu and Susanowo (based on the Kojiki)]]
'''Kumanokusubi''' (熊野久须毘命,熊野櫲樟日命, Wonder Worker of Bear Moors)<ref name="a-history-of-japan-日本歴史-2021a" /> is a [[Kami|God]] in [[Japanese mythology]]. He is the fifth son of [[Amaterasu]].<ref name="a-history-of-japan-日本歴史-2021a">{{Cite web|title=Kumanokusubi • A History of Japan - 日本歴史|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/kumanokusubi/|access-date=2021-09-28|website=A History of Japan - 日本歴史|language=en-GB}}</ref><ref>{{cite web | url=https://archive.today/20230320012745/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9369 | title=Kumanokusubi | 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム }}</ref>
Some scholars have identified this ''[[kami]]'' as the ''saijin'' at the shrine [[Kumano Jinja]] in Shimane Prefecture.<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2021a">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9369 |archive-url= https://web.archive.org/web/20230214183150/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9369|access-date=2021-09-28 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム | archive-date=2023-02-14 |language=ja}}</ref>
== Name == He goes by other names like '''Kumano no oshihomi no mikoto''', '''Kumano no oshikuma no mikoto''', '''Kumano no oshisumi no mikoto''', and '''Kumano no osumi no mikoto.'''<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2021a" />
== Summary == He was born out of a kami-making competition between Amaterasu and [[Susanoo]].
In many versions, Susanoo took Amaterasu's beads and crushed them within his mouth, which created five male kami.<ref>{{cite book |author=Seigo Takahashi |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=H18tAAAAYAAJ&dq=Amaterasu+and+Susanoo+oath&pg=PA66 |title=A Study of the Origin of the Japanese State |publisher=W. D. Gray |year=1917}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Amenooshihomimi • A History of Japan - 日本歴史 |url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/amenooshihomimi/ |access-date=2020-09-28 |website=A History of Japan - 日本歴史 |language=en-GB}}</ref> The first one to be born was [[Ame-no-oshihomimi|Amenooshihomimi]], second was [[Ame-no-hohi]], third was [[Amatsuhikone]], fourth was [[Ikutsuhikone]], and [[Kumanokusubi]] was the fifth.<ref>{{Cite book |last1=Havens |first1=Norman |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a_YQAQAAIAAJ&q=Amenooshihomimi |title=An Encyclopedia of Shinto (Shinto Jiten): Kami |last2=Inoue |first2=Nobutaka |date=2006 |publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics Kokugakuin University |isbn=978-4-905853-08-4 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Shinto Portal - IJCC, Kokugakuin University |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=33}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |year=1990 |title=The Journal of the Association of Teachers of Japanese |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Q5cqAQAAIAAJ&q=Amatsuhikone}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Kumanokusubi |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=99 |access-date=2020-10-07 |website=eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp}}</ref>
== Related to Kumano ==
The deity's name, Kusubi (Kusuhi), is thought to mean "strange spirit" (mysterious divine spirit) or "strange fire. The current deity of Kumano-taisha is "Kumano-taishin Kushimikino- no-mikoto," but there is a theory that the original deity was Kumanokusubi.
Kumano-taishin Kushimikino- no-mikoto," but there is a theory that the original deity was Kumanokusubi. There is a theory that the deity of [[Kumano Nachi Taisha]], Kumanokusubi, is [[Izanami|Izamiami]], but this is also believed to be a reference to Kumanokusubi.<ref>{{Cite book|title="Nihon no kami yomi kakkai jiten"|publisher=Kashiwa Shobo|others=Kawaguchi Kenji (ed.)|year=1999|isbn=978-4-7601-1824-3|language=ja}}</ref>}

'''Kumanokusubi''' (熊野久须毘命,熊野櫲樟日命, Wonder Worker of Bear Moors)<ref name="a-history-of-japan-日本歴史-2021a" /> is a [[Kami|God]] in [[Japanese mythology]]. He is the fifth son of [[Amaterasu]].<ref name="a-history-of-japan-日本歴史-2021a">{{Cite web|title=Kumanokusubi • A History of Japan - 日本歴史|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/kumanokusubi/|access-date=2021-09-28|website=A History of Japan - 日本歴史|language=en-GB}}</ref><ref>{{cite web | url=https://archive.today/20230320012745/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9369 | title=Kumanokusubi | 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム }}</ref>
Some scholars have identified this ''[[kami]]'' as the ''saijin'' at the shrine [[Kumano Jinja]] in Shimane Prefecture.<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2021a">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9369 |archive-url= https://web.archive.org/web/20230214183150/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9369|access-date=2021-09-28 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム | archive-date=2023-02-14 |language=ja}}</ref>
== Name == He goes by other names like '''Kumano no oshihomi no mikoto''', '''Kumano no oshikuma no mikoto''', '''Kumano no oshisumi no mikoto''', and '''Kumano no osumi no mikoto.'''<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2021a" />
== Summary == He was born out of a kami-making competition between Amaterasu and [[Susanoo]].
In many versions, Susanoo took Amaterasu's beads and crushed them within his mouth, which created five male kami.<ref>{{cite book |author=Seigo Takahashi |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=H18tAAAAYAAJ&dq=Amaterasu+and+Susanoo+oath&pg=PA66 |title=A Study of the Origin of the Japanese State |publisher=W. D. Gray |year=1917}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Amenooshihomimi • A History of Japan - 日本歴史 |url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/amenooshihomimi/ |access-date=2020-09-28 |website=A History of Japan - 日本歴史 |language=en-GB}}</ref> The first one to be born was [[Ame-no-oshihomimi|Amenooshihomimi]], second was [[Ame-no-hohi]], third was [[Amatsuhikone]], fourth was [[Ikutsuhikone]], and [[Kumanokusubi]] was the fifth.<ref>{{Cite book |last1=Havens |first1=Norman |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a_YQAQAAIAAJ&q=Amenooshihomimi |title=An Encyclopedia of Shinto (Shinto Jiten): Kami |last2=Inoue |first2=Nobutaka |date=2006 |publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics Kokugakuin University |isbn=978-4-905853-08-4 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Shinto Portal - IJCC, Kokugakuin University |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=33}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |year=1990 |title=The Journal of the Association of Teachers of Japanese |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Q5cqAQAAIAAJ&q=Amatsuhikone}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Kumanokusubi |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=99 |access-date=2020-10-07 |website=eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp}}</ref>
== Related to Kumano ==
The deity's name, Kusubi (Kusuhi), is thought to mean "strange spirit" (mysterious divine spirit) or "strange fire. The current deity of Kumano-taisha is "Kumano-taishin Kushimikino- no-mikoto," but there is a theory that the original deity was Kumanokusubi.
Kumano-taishin Kushimikino- no-mikoto," but there is a theory that the original deity was Kumanokusubi. There is a theory that the deity of [[Kumano Nachi Taisha]], Kumanokusubi, is [[Izanami|Izamiami]], but this is also believed to be a reference to Kumanokusubi.<ref>{{Cite book|title="Nihon no kami yomi kakkai jiten"|publisher=Kashiwa Shobo|others=Kawaguchi Kenji (ed.)|year=1999|isbn=978-4-7601-1824-3|language=ja}}</ref>}

'''Kumanokusubi''' (熊野久须毘命,熊野櫲樟日命, Wonder Worker of Bear Moors)<ref name="a-history-of-japan-日本歴史-2021a" /> is a [[Kami|God]] in [[Japanese mythology]]. He is the fifth son of [[Amaterasu]].<ref name="a-history-of-japan-日本歴史-2021a">{{Cite web|title=Kumanokusubi • A History of Japan - 日本歴史|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/kumanokusubi/|access-date=2021-09-28|website=A History of Japan - 日本歴史|language=en-GB}}</ref><ref>{{cite web | url=https://archive.today/20230320012745/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9369 | title=Kumanokusubi | 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム }}</ref>
Some scholars have identified this ''[[kami]]'' as the ''saijin'' at the shrine [[Kumano Jinja]] in Shimane Prefecture.<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2021a">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9369 |archive-url= https://web.archive.org/web/20230214183150/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9369|access-date=2021-09-28 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム | archive-date=2023-02-14 |language=ja}}</ref>
== Name == He goes by other names like '''Kumano no oshihomi no mikoto''', '''Kumano no oshikuma no mikoto''', '''Kumano no oshisumi no mikoto''', and '''Kumano no osumi no mikoto.'''<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2021a" />
== Summary == He was born out of a kami-making competition between Amaterasu and [[Susanoo]].
In many versions, Susanoo took Amaterasu's beads and crushed them within his mouth, which created five male kami.<ref>{{cite book |author=Seigo Takahashi |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=H18tAAAAYAAJ&dq=Amaterasu+and+Susanoo+oath&pg=PA66 |title=A Study of the Origin of the Japanese State |publisher=W. D. Gray |year=1917}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Amenooshihomimi • A History of Japan - 日本歴史 |url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/amenooshihomimi/ |access-date=2020-09-28 |website=A History of Japan - 日本歴史 |language=en-GB}}</ref> The first one to be born was [[Ame-no-oshihomimi|Amenooshihomimi]], second was [[Ame-no-hohi]], third was [[Amatsuhikone]], fourth was [[Ikutsuhikone]], and [[Kumanokusubi]] was the fifth.<ref>{{Cite book |last1=Havens |first1=Norman |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a_YQAQAAIAAJ&q=Amenooshihomimi |title=An Encyclopedia of Shinto (Shinto Jiten): Kami |last2=Inoue |first2=Nobutaka |date=2006 |publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics Kokugakuin University |isbn=978-4-905853-08-4 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Shinto Portal - IJCC, Kokugakuin University |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=33}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |year=1990 |title=The Journal of the Association of Teachers of Japanese |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Q5cqAQAAIAAJ&q=Amatsuhikone}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Kumanokusubi |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=99 |access-date=2020-10-07 |website=eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp}}</ref>
== Related to Kumano ==
The deity's name, Kusubi (Kusuhi), is thought to mean "strange spirit" (mysterious divine spirit) or "strange fire. The current deity of Kumano-taisha is "Kumano-taishin Kushimikino- no-mikoto," but there is a theory that the original deity was Kumanokusubi.
Kumano-taishin Kushimikino- no-mikoto," but there is a theory that the original deity was Kumanokusubi. There is a theory that the deity of [[Kumano Nachi Taisha]], Kumanokusubi, is [[Izanami|Izamiami]], but this is also believed to be a reference to Kumanokusubi.<ref>{{Cite book|title="Nihon no kami yomi kakkai jiten"|publisher=Kashiwa Shobo|others=Kawaguchi Kenji (ed.)|year=1999|isbn=978-4-7601-1824-3|language=ja}}</ref>}
