<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kumanokusubi]][[ja:クマノクスビ]][[ru:Куманокусуби]][[zh:熊野久須毘命]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11298796]] Wikidata name: Kumanokusubi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:43, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|God in Japanese mythology}} {{refimprove|date=January 2022}} {{Infobox deity | type = Shinto | name = Kumanokusubi | siblings = [[Ame no Hohi]] (brother) [[:en:Ame-no-oshihomimi|Ame-no-oshihomimi]] (brother) [[:en:Ikutsuhikone|Ikutsuhikone]] (brother) [[Amatsuhikone]] (brother) | mother = [[Amaterasu]] | image = File:Kumanokusubi shinbutsu-zue.jpg | gender = {{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}} | parents ={{ill|Susanoo|WD=Q272993}}; {{ill|Amaterasu|WD=Q455602}}| equivalent5_type = Japanese | equivalent5 = {{ill|Hyōbijin|WD=Q60148}} }}[[File:Children born from the Challenge of Amaterasu and Susanoo-en.svg|thumb|240x240px|Oath between Amaterasu and Susanowo (based on the Kojiki)]]
'''Kumanokusubi''' (熊野久须毘命,熊野櫲樟日命, Wonder Worker of Bear Moors)<ref name="a-history-of-japan-日本歴史-2021a" /> is a [[Kami|God]] in [[Japanese mythology]]. He is the fifth son of [[Amaterasu]].<ref name="a-history-of-japan-日本歴史-2021a">{{Cite web|title=Kumanokusubi • A History of Japan - 日本歴史|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/kumanokusubi/|access-date=2021-09-28|website=A History of Japan - 日本歴史|language=en-GB}}</ref><ref>{{cite web | url=https://archive.today/20230320012745/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9369 | title=Kumanokusubi | 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム }}</ref>
Some scholars have identified this ''[[kami]]'' as the ''saijin'' at the shrine [[:en:Kumano Jinja|Kumano Jinja]] in Shimane Prefecture.<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2021a">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9369 |archive-url= https://web.archive.org/web/20230214183150/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9369|access-date=2021-09-28 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム | archive-date=2023-02-14 |language=ja}}</ref>
== Name == He goes by other names like '''Kumano no oshihomi no mikoto''', '''Kumano no oshikuma no mikoto''', '''Kumano no oshisumi no mikoto''', and '''Kumano no osumi no mikoto.'''<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2021a" />
== Summary == He was born out of a kami-making competition between Amaterasu and [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]].
In many versions, Susanoo took Amaterasu's beads and crushed them within his mouth, which created five male kami.<ref>{{cite book |author=Seigo Takahashi |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=H18tAAAAYAAJ&dq=Amaterasu+and+Susanoo+oath&pg=PA66 |title=A Study of the Origin of the Japanese State |publisher=W. D. Gray |year=1917}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Amenooshihomimi • A History of Japan - 日本歴史 |url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/amenooshihomimi/ |access-date=2020-09-28 |website=A History of Japan - 日本歴史 |language=en-GB}}</ref> The first one to be born was [[:en:Ame-no-oshihomimi|Amenooshihomimi]], second was [[Ame no Hohi]], third was [[Amatsuhikone]], fourth was [[:en:Ikutsuhikone|Ikutsuhikone]], and [[Kumanokusubi]] was the fifth.<ref>{{Cite book |last1=Havens |first1=Norman |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a_YQAQAAIAAJ&q=Amenooshihomimi |title=An Encyclopedia of Shinto (Shinto Jiten): Kami |last2=Inoue |first2=Nobutaka |date=2006 |publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics Kokugakuin University |isbn=978-4-905853-08-4 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Shinto Portal - IJCC, Kokugakuin University |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=33}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |year=1990 |title=The Journal of the Association of Teachers of Japanese |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Q5cqAQAAIAAJ&q=Amatsuhikone}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Kumanokusubi |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=99 |access-date=2020-10-07 |website=eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp}}</ref>
== Related to Kumano ==
The deity's name, Kusubi (Kusuhi), is thought to mean "strange spirit" (mysterious divine spirit) or "strange fire. The current deity of Kumano-taisha is "Kumano-taishin Kushimikino- no-mikoto," but there is a theory that the original deity was Kumanokusubi.
Kumano-taishin Kushimikino- no-mikoto," but there is a theory that the original deity was Kumanokusubi. There is a theory that the deity of [[Kumano Nachi Taisha]], Kumanokusubi, is [[:en:Izanami|Izamiami]], but this is also believed to be a reference to Kumanokusubi.<ref>{{Cite book|title="Nihon no kami yomi kakkai jiten"|publisher=Kashiwa Shobo|others=Kawaguchi Kenji (ed.)|year=1999|isbn=978-4-7601-1824-3|language=ja}}</ref>}

Kumano-taishin Kushimikino- no-mikoto," but there is a theory that the original deity was Kumanokusubi. There is a theory that the deity of [[Kumano Nachi Taisha]], Kumanokusubi, is [[:en:Izanami|Izamiami]], but this is also believed to be a reference to Kumanokusubi.<ref>{{Cite book|title="Nihon no kami yomi kakkai jiten"|publisher=Kashiwa Shobo|others=Kawaguchi Kenji (ed.)|year=1999|isbn=978-4-7601-1824-3|language=ja}}</ref>}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kumanokusubi]][[ja:クマノクスビ]][[ru:Куманокусуби]][[zh:熊野久須毘命]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kumanokusubi]][[ja:クマノクスビ]][[ru:Куманокусуби]][[zh:熊野久須毘命]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11298796]] Wikidata name: Kumanokusubi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:43, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|God in Japanese mythology}} {{refimprove|date=January 2022}} {{Infobox deity | type = Shinto | name = Kumanokusubi | siblings = [[Ame no Hohi]] (brother) [[:en:Ame-no-oshihomimi|Ame-no-oshihomimi]] (brother) [[:en:Ikutsuhikone|Ikutsuhikone]] (brother) [[Amatsuhikone]] (brother) | mother = [[Amaterasu]] | image = File:Kumanokusubi shinbutsu-zue.jpg | gender = {{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}} | parents ={{ill|Susanoo|WD=Q272993}}; {{ill|Amaterasu|WD=Q455602}}| equivalent5_type = Japanese | equivalent5 = {{ill|Hyōbijin|WD=Q60148}} }}[[File:Children born from the Challenge of Amaterasu and Susanoo-en.svg|thumb|240x240px|Oath between Amaterasu and Susanowo (based on the Kojiki)]]
'''Kumanokusubi''' (熊野久须毘命,熊野櫲樟日命, Wonder Worker of Bear Moors)<ref name="a-history-of-japan-日本歴史-2021a" /> is a [[Kami|God]] in [[Japanese mythology]]. He is the fifth son of [[Amaterasu]].<ref name="a-history-of-japan-日本歴史-2021a">{{Cite web|title=Kumanokusubi • A History of Japan - 日本歴史|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/kumanokusubi/|access-date=2021-09-28|website=A History of Japan - 日本歴史|language=en-GB}}</ref><ref>{{cite web | url=https://archive.today/20230320012745/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9369 | title=Kumanokusubi | 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム }}</ref>
Some scholars have identified this ''[[kami]]'' as the ''saijin'' at the shrine [[:en:Kumano Jinja|Kumano Jinja]] in Shimane Prefecture.<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2021a">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9369 |archive-url= https://web.archive.org/web/20230214183150/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9369|access-date=2021-09-28 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム | archive-date=2023-02-14 |language=ja}}</ref>
== Name == He goes by other names like '''Kumano no oshihomi no mikoto''', '''Kumano no oshikuma no mikoto''', '''Kumano no oshisumi no mikoto''', and '''Kumano no osumi no mikoto.'''<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2021a" />
== Summary == He was born out of a kami-making competition between Amaterasu and [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]].
In many versions, Susanoo took Amaterasu's beads and crushed them within his mouth, which created five male kami.<ref>{{cite book |author=Seigo Takahashi |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=H18tAAAAYAAJ&dq=Amaterasu+and+Susanoo+oath&pg=PA66 |title=A Study of the Origin of the Japanese State |publisher=W. D. Gray |year=1917}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Amenooshihomimi • A History of Japan - 日本歴史 |url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/amenooshihomimi/ |access-date=2020-09-28 |website=A History of Japan - 日本歴史 |language=en-GB}}</ref> The first one to be born was [[:en:Ame-no-oshihomimi|Amenooshihomimi]], second was [[Ame no Hohi]], third was [[Amatsuhikone]], fourth was [[:en:Ikutsuhikone|Ikutsuhikone]], and [[Kumanokusubi]] was the fifth.<ref>{{Cite book |last1=Havens |first1=Norman |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a_YQAQAAIAAJ&q=Amenooshihomimi |title=An Encyclopedia of Shinto (Shinto Jiten): Kami |last2=Inoue |first2=Nobutaka |date=2006 |publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics Kokugakuin University |isbn=978-4-905853-08-4 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Shinto Portal - IJCC, Kokugakuin University |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=33}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |year=1990 |title=The Journal of the Association of Teachers of Japanese |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Q5cqAQAAIAAJ&q=Amatsuhikone}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Kumanokusubi |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=99 |access-date=2020-10-07 |website=eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp}}</ref>
== Related to Kumano ==
The deity's name, Kusubi (Kusuhi), is thought to mean "strange spirit" (mysterious divine spirit) or "strange fire. The current deity of Kumano-taisha is "Kumano-taishin Kushimikino- no-mikoto," but there is a theory that the original deity was Kumanokusubi.
Kumano-taishin Kushimikino- no-mikoto," but there is a theory that the original deity was Kumanokusubi. There is a theory that the deity of [[Kumano Nachi Taisha]], Kumanokusubi, is [[:en:Izanami|Izamiami]], but this is also believed to be a reference to Kumanokusubi.<ref>{{Cite book|title="Nihon no kami yomi kakkai jiten"|publisher=Kashiwa Shobo|others=Kawaguchi Kenji (ed.)|year=1999|isbn=978-4-7601-1824-3|language=ja}}</ref>}

Gray |year=1917}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Amenooshihomimi • A History of Japan - 日本歴史 |url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/amenooshihomimi/ |access-date=2020-09-28 |website=A History of Japan - 日本歴史 |language=en-GB}}</ref> The first one to be born was [[:en:Ame-no-oshihomimi|Amenooshihomimi]], second was [[Ame no Hohi]], third was [[Amatsuhikone]], fourth was [[:en:Ikutsuhikone|Ikutsuhikone]], and [[Kumanokusubi]] was the fifth.<ref>{{Cite book |last1=Havens |first1=Norman |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a_YQAQAAIAAJ&q=Amenooshihomimi |title=An Encyclopedia of Shinto (Shinto Jiten): Kami |last2=Inoue |first2=Nobutaka |date=2006 |publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics Kokugakuin University |isbn=978-4-905853-08-4 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Shinto Portal - IJCC, Kokugakuin University |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=33}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |year=1990 |title=The Journal of the Association of Teachers of Japanese |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Q5cqAQAAIAAJ&q=Amatsuhikone}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Kumanokusubi |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=99 |access-date=2020-10-07 |website=eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp}}</ref>
== Related to Kumano ==
The deity's name, Kusubi (Kusuhi), is thought to mean "strange spirit" (mysterious divine spirit) or "strange fire. The current deity of Kumano-taisha is "Kumano-taishin Kushimikino- no-mikoto," but there is a theory that the original deity was Kumanokusubi.
Kumano-taishin Kushimikino- no-mikoto," but there is a theory that the original deity was Kumanokusubi. There is a theory that the deity of [[Kumano Nachi Taisha]], Kumanokusubi, is [[:en:Izanami|Izamiami]], but this is also believed to be a reference to Kumanokusubi.<ref>{{Cite book|title="Nihon no kami yomi kakkai jiten"|publisher=Kashiwa Shobo|others=Kawaguchi Kenji (ed.)|year=1999|isbn=978-4-7601-1824-3|language=ja}}</ref>}

He is the fifth son of [[Amaterasu]].<ref name="a-history-of-japan-日本歴史-2021a">{{Cite web|title=Kumanokusubi • A History of Japan - 日本歴史|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/kumanokusubi/|access-date=2021-09-28|website=A History of Japan - 日本歴史|language=en-GB}}</ref><ref>{{cite web | url=https://archive.today/20230320012745/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9369 | title=Kumanokusubi | 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム }}</ref>
Some scholars have identified this ''[[kami]]'' as the ''saijin'' at the shrine [[:en:Kumano Jinja|Kumano Jinja]] in Shimane Prefecture.<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2021a">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9369 |archive-url= https://web.archive.org/web/20230214183150/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9369|access-date=2021-09-28 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム | archive-date=2023-02-14 |language=ja}}</ref>
== Name == He goes by other names like '''Kumano no oshihomi no mikoto''', '''Kumano no oshikuma no mikoto''', '''Kumano no oshisumi no mikoto''', and '''Kumano no osumi no mikoto.'''<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2021a" />
== Summary == He was born out of a kami-making competition between Amaterasu and [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]].
In many versions, Susanoo took Amaterasu's beads and crushed them within his mouth, which created five male kami.<ref>{{cite book |author=Seigo Takahashi |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=H18tAAAAYAAJ&dq=Amaterasu+and+Susanoo+oath&pg=PA66 |title=A Study of the Origin of the Japanese State |publisher=W. D. Gray |year=1917}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Amenooshihomimi • A History of Japan - 日本歴史 |url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/amenooshihomimi/ |access-date=2020-09-28 |website=A History of Japan - 日本歴史 |language=en-GB}}</ref> The first one to be born was {{ill|Ame-no-oshihomimi|en|Ame-no-oshihomimi|WD=Q462555|lt=Amenooshihomimi}}, second was [[Ame no Hohi]], third was [[Amatsuhikone]], fourth was {{ill|Ikutsuhikone|en|Ikutsuhikone|WD=Q11147583|lt=Ikutsuhikone}}, and [[Kumanokusubi]] was the fifth.<ref>{{Cite book |last1=Havens |first1=Norman |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a_YQAQAAIAAJ&q=Amenooshihomimi |title=An Encyclopedia of Shinto (Shinto Jiten): Kami |last2=Inoue |first2=Nobutaka |date=2006 |publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics Kokugakuin University |isbn=978-4-905853-08-4 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Shinto Portal - IJCC, Kokugakuin University |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=33}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |year=1990 |title=The Journal of the Association of Teachers of Japanese |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Q5cqAQAAIAAJ&q=Amatsuhikone}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Kumanokusubi |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=99 |access-date=2020-10-07 |website=eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp}}</ref>
== Related to Kumano ==
The deity's name, Kusubi (Kusuhi), is thought to mean "strange spirit" (mysterious divine spirit) or "strange fire. The current deity of Kumano-taisha is "Kumano-taishin Kushimikino- no-mikoto," but there is a theory that the original deity was Kumanokusubi.
Kumano-taishin Kushimikino- no-mikoto," but there is a theory that the original deity was Kumanokusubi. There is a theory that the deity of [[Kumano Nachi Taisha]], Kumanokusubi, is {{ill|Izanami|en|Izanami|WD=Q682306|lt=Izamiami}}, but this is also believed to be a reference to Kumanokusubi.<ref>{{Cite book|title="Nihon no kami yomi kakkai jiten"|publisher=Kashiwa Shobo|others=Kawaguchi Kenji (ed.)|year=1999|isbn=978-4-7601-1824-3|language=ja}}</ref>}

D. Gray |year=1917}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Amenooshihomimi • A History of Japan - 日本歴史 |url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/amenooshihomimi/ |access-date=2020-09-28 |website=A History of Japan - 日本歴史 |language=en-GB}}</ref> The first one to be born was {{ill|Ame-no-oshihomimi|en|Ame-no-oshihomimi|WD=Q462555|lt=Amenooshihomimi}}, second was [[Ame no Hohi]], third was [[Amatsuhikone]], fourth was {{ill|Ikutsuhikone|en|Ikutsuhikone|WD=Q11147583|lt=Ikutsuhikone}}, and [[Kumanokusubi]] was the fifth.<ref>{{Cite book |last1=Havens |first1=Norman |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a_YQAQAAIAAJ&q=Amenooshihomimi |title=An Encyclopedia of Shinto (Shinto Jiten): Kami |last2=Inoue |first2=Nobutaka |date=2006 |publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics Kokugakuin University |isbn=978-4-905853-08-4 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Shinto Portal - IJCC, Kokugakuin University |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=33}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |year=1990 |title=The Journal of the Association of Teachers of Japanese |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Q5cqAQAAIAAJ&q=Amatsuhikone}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Kumanokusubi |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=99 |access-date=2020-10-07 |website=eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp}}</ref>
== Related to Kumano ==
The deity's name, Kusubi (Kusuhi), is thought to mean "strange spirit" (mysterious divine spirit) or "strange fire. The current deity of Kumano-taisha is "Kumano-taishin Kushimikino- no-mikoto," but there is a theory that the original deity was Kumanokusubi.
Kumano-taishin Kushimikino- no-mikoto," but there is a theory that the original deity was Kumanokusubi. There is a theory that the deity of [[Kumano Nachi Taisha]], Kumanokusubi, is {{ill|Izanami|en|Izanami|WD=Q682306|lt=Izamiami}}, but this is also believed to be a reference to Kumanokusubi.<ref>{{Cite book|title="Nihon no kami yomi kakkai jiten"|publisher=Kashiwa Shobo|others=Kawaguchi Kenji (ed.)|year=1999|isbn=978-4-7601-1824-3|language=ja}}</ref>}

D. Gray |year=1917}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Amenooshihomimi • A History of Japan - 日本歴史 |url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/amenooshihomimi/ |access-date=2020-09-28 |website=A History of Japan - 日本歴史 |language=en-GB}}</ref> The first one to be born was {{ill|Ame-no-oshihomimi|en|Ame-no-oshihomimi|WD=Q462555|lt=Amenooshihomimi}}, second was [[Ame no Hohi]], third was [[Amatsuhikone]], fourth was {{ill|Ikutsuhikone|en|Ikutsuhikone|WD=Q11147583|lt=Ikutsuhikone}}, and [[Kumanokusubi]] was the fifth.<ref>{{Cite book |last1=Havens |first1=Norman |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a_YQAQAAIAAJ&q=Amenooshihomimi |title=An Encyclopedia of Shinto (Shinto Jiten): Kami |last2=Inoue |first2=Nobutaka |date=2006 |publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics Kokugakuin University |isbn=978-4-905853-08-4 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Shinto Portal - IJCC, Kokugakuin University |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=33}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |year=1990 |title=The Journal of the Association of Teachers of Japanese |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Q5cqAQAAIAAJ&q=Amatsuhikone}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Kumanokusubi |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=99 |access-date=2020-10-07 |website=eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp}}</ref>

{{Short description|God in Japanese mythology}} {{refimprove|date=January 2022}} {{Infobox deity | type = Shinto | name = Kumanokusubi | siblings = [[Ame no Hohi]] (brother) {{ill|Ame-no-oshihomimi|en|Ame-no-oshihomimi|WD=Q462555|lt=Ame-no-oshihomimi|1=Ame-no-oshihomimi}} (brother) {{ill|Ikutsuhikone|en|Ikutsuhikone|WD=Q11147583|lt=Ikutsuhikone|1=Ikutsuhikone}} (brother) [[Amatsuhikone]] (brother) | mother = [[Amaterasu]] | image = File:Kumanokusubi shinbutsu-zue.jpg | gender = {{ill|male|WD=Q6581097|1=Male (gender)}} | parents ={{ill|Susanoo|WD=Q272993|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}; {{ill|Amaterasu|WD=Q455602|1=Amaterasu}}| equivalent5_type = Japanese | equivalent5 = {{ill|Hyōbijin|WD=Q60148|1=Hyōbijin}} }}[[File:Children born from the Challenge of Amaterasu and Susanoo-en.svg|thumb|240x240px|Oath between Amaterasu and Susanowo (based on the Kojiki)]]
'''Kumanokusubi''' (熊野久须毘命,熊野櫲樟日命, Wonder Worker of Bear Moors)<ref name="a-history-of-japan-日本歴史-2021a" /> is a [[Kami|God]] in [[Japanese mythology]]. He is the fifth son of [[Amaterasu]].<ref name="a-history-of-japan-日本歴史-2021a">{{Cite web|title=Kumanokusubi • A History of Japan - 日本歴史|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/kumanokusubi/|access-date=2021-09-28|website=A History of Japan - 日本歴史|language=en-GB}}</ref><ref>{{cite web | url=https://archive.today/20230320012745/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9369 | title=Kumanokusubi | 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム }}</ref>
Some scholars have identified this ''[[kami]]'' as the ''saijin'' at the shrine [[:en:Kumano Jinja|Kumano Jinja]] in Shimane Prefecture.<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2021a">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9369 |archive-url= https://web.archive.org/web/20230214183150/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9369|access-date=2021-09-28 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム | archive-date=2023-02-14 |language=ja}}</ref>
== Name == He goes by other names like '''Kumano no oshihomi no mikoto''', '''Kumano no oshikuma no mikoto''', '''Kumano no oshisumi no mikoto''', and '''Kumano no osumi no mikoto.'''<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2021a" />
== Summary == He was born out of a kami-making competition between Amaterasu and [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]].
In many versions, Susanoo took Amaterasu's beads and crushed them within his mouth, which created five male kami.<ref>{{cite book |author=Seigo Takahashi |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=H18tAAAAYAAJ&dq=Amaterasu+and+Susanoo+oath&pg=PA66 |title=A Study of the Origin of the Japanese State |publisher=W. D. Gray |year=1917}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Amenooshihomimi • A History of Japan - 日本歴史 |url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/amenooshihomimi/ |access-date=2020-09-28 |website=A History of Japan - 日本歴史 |language=en-GB}}</ref> The first one to be born was {{ill|Ame-no-oshihomimi|en|Ame-no-oshihomimi|WD=Q462555|lt=Amenooshihomimi|1=Ame-no-oshihomimi}}, second was [[Ame no Hohi]], third was [[Amatsuhikone]], fourth was {{ill|Ikutsuhikone|en|Ikutsuhikone|WD=Q11147583|lt=Ikutsuhikone|1=Ikutsuhikone}}, and [[Kumanokusubi]] was the fifth.<ref>{{Cite book |last1=Havens |first1=Norman |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a_YQAQAAIAAJ&q=Amenooshihomimi |title=An Encyclopedia of Shinto (Shinto Jiten): Kami |last2=Inoue |first2=Nobutaka |date=2006 |publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics Kokugakuin University |isbn=978-4-905853-08-4 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Shinto Portal - IJCC, Kokugakuin University |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=33}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |year=1990 |title=The Journal of the Association of Teachers of Japanese |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Q5cqAQAAIAAJ&q=Amatsuhikone}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Kumanokusubi |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=99 |access-date=2020-10-07 |website=eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp}}</ref>
== Related to Kumano ==
The deity's name, Kusubi (Kusuhi), is thought to mean "strange spirit" (mysterious divine spirit) or "strange fire. The current deity of Kumano-taisha is "Kumano-taishin Kushimikino- no-mikoto," but there is a theory that the original deity was Kumanokusubi.
Kumano-taishin Kushimikino- no-mikoto," but there is a theory that the original deity was Kumanokusubi. There is a theory that the deity of [[Kumano Nachi Taisha]], Kumanokusubi, is {{ill|Izanami|en|Izanami|WD=Q682306|lt=Izamiami|1=Izanami}}, but this is also believed to be a reference to Kumanokusubi.<ref>{{Cite book|title="Nihon no kami yomi kakkai jiten"|publisher=Kashiwa Shobo|others=Kawaguchi Kenji (ed.)|year=1999|isbn=978-4-7601-1824-3|language=ja}}</ref>}

Gray |year=1917}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Amenooshihomimi • A History of Japan - 日本歴史 |url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/amenooshihomimi/ |access-date=2020-09-28 |website=A History of Japan - 日本歴史 |language=en-GB}}</ref> The first one to be born was {{ill|Ame-no-oshihomimi|en|Ame-no-oshihomimi|WD=Q462555|lt=Amenooshihomimi|1=Ame-no-oshihomimi}}, second was [[Ame no Hohi]], third was [[Amatsuhikone]], fourth was {{ill|Ikutsuhikone|en|Ikutsuhikone|WD=Q11147583|lt=Ikutsuhikone|1=Ikutsuhikone}}, and [[Kumanokusubi]] was the fifth.<ref>{{Cite book |last1=Havens |first1=Norman |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a_YQAQAAIAAJ&q=Amenooshihomimi |title=An Encyclopedia of Shinto (Shinto Jiten): Kami |last2=Inoue |first2=Nobutaka |date=2006 |publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics Kokugakuin University |isbn=978-4-905853-08-4 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Shinto Portal - IJCC, Kokugakuin University |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=33}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |year=1990 |title=The Journal of the Association of Teachers of Japanese |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Q5cqAQAAIAAJ&q=Amatsuhikone}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Kumanokusubi |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=99 |access-date=2020-10-07 |website=eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp}}</ref>

D. Gray |year=1917}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Amenooshihomimi • A History of Japan - 日本歴史 |url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/amenooshihomimi/ |access-date=2020-09-28 |website=A History of Japan - 日本歴史 |language=en-GB}}</ref> The first one to be born was {{ill|Ame-no-oshihomimi|en|Ame-no-oshihomimi|WD=Q462555|lt=Amenooshihomimi|1=Ame-no-oshihomimi}}, second was [[Ame no Hohi]], third was [[Amatsuhikone]], fourth was {{ill|Ikutsuhikone|en|Ikutsuhikone|WD=Q11147583|lt=Ikutsuhikone|1=Ikutsuhikone}}, and [[Kumanokusubi]] was the fifth.<ref>{{Cite book |last1=Havens |first1=Norman |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a_YQAQAAIAAJ&q=Amenooshihomimi |title=An Encyclopedia of Shinto (Shinto Jiten): Kami |last2=Inoue |first2=Nobutaka |date=2006 |publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics Kokugakuin University |isbn=978-4-905853-08-4 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Shinto Portal - IJCC, Kokugakuin University |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=33}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |year=1990 |title=The Journal of the Association of Teachers of Japanese |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Q5cqAQAAIAAJ&q=Amatsuhikone}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Kumanokusubi |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=99 |access-date=2020-10-07 |website=eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp}}</ref>
== Related to Kumano ==
The deity's name, Kusubi (Kusuhi), is thought to mean "strange spirit" (mysterious divine spirit) or "strange fire. The current deity of Kumano-taisha is "Kumano-taishin Kushimikino- no-mikoto," but there is a theory that the original deity was Kumanokusubi.
Kumano-taishin Kushimikino- no-mikoto," but there is a theory that the original deity was Kumanokusubi. There is a theory that the deity of [[Kumano Nachi Taisha]], Kumanokusubi, is {{ill|Izanami|en|Izanami|WD=Q682306|lt=Izamiami|1=Izanami}}, but this is also believed to be a reference to Kumanokusubi.<ref>{{Cite book|title="Nihon no kami yomi kakkai jiten"|publisher=Kashiwa Shobo|others=Kawaguchi Kenji (ed.)|year=1999|isbn=978-4-7601-1824-3|language=ja}}</ref>}

{{Short description|God in Japanese mythology}} {{refimprove|date=January 2022}} {{Infobox deity | type = Shinto | name = Kumanokusubi | siblings = [[Ame no Hohi]] (brother) {{ill|Ame-no-oshihomimi|en|Ame-no-oshihomimi|qid=Q462555|lt=Ame-no-oshihomimi|1=Ame-no-oshihomimi}} (brother) {{ill|Ikutsuhikone|en|Ikutsuhikone|qid=Q11147583|lt=Ikutsuhikone|1=Ikutsuhikone}} (brother) [[Amatsuhikone]] (brother) | mother = [[Amaterasu]] | image = File:Kumanokusubi shinbutsu-zue.jpg | gender = {{ill|male|qid=Q6581097|1=Male (gender)}} | parents ={{ill|Susanoo|qid=Q272993|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}; {{ill|Amaterasu|qid=Q455602|1=Amaterasu}}| equivalent5_type = Japanese | equivalent5 = {{ill|Hyōbijin|qid=Q60148|1=Hyōbijin}} }}[[File:Children born from the Challenge of Amaterasu and Susanoo-en.svg|thumb|240x240px|Oath between Amaterasu and Susanowo (based on the Kojiki)]]
'''Kumanokusubi''' (熊野久须毘命,熊野櫲樟日命, Wonder Worker of Bear Moors)<ref name="a-history-of-japan-日本歴史-2021a" /> is a [[Kami|God]] in [[Japanese mythology]]. He is the fifth son of [[Amaterasu]].<ref name="a-history-of-japan-日本歴史-2021a">{{Cite web|title=Kumanokusubi • A History of Japan - 日本歴史|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/kumanokusubi/|access-date=2021-09-28|website=A History of Japan - 日本歴史|language=en-GB}}</ref><ref>{{cite web | url=https://archive.today/20230320012745/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9369 | title=Kumanokusubi | 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム }}</ref>
Some scholars have identified this ''[[kami]]'' as the ''saijin'' at the shrine [[:en:Kumano Jinja|Kumano Jinja]] in Shimane Prefecture.<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2021a">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9369 |archive-url= https://web.archive.org/web/20230214183150/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9369|access-date=2021-09-28 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム | archive-date=2023-02-14 |language=ja}}</ref>
== Name == He goes by other names like '''Kumano no oshihomi no mikoto''', '''Kumano no oshikuma no mikoto''', '''Kumano no oshisumi no mikoto''', and '''Kumano no osumi no mikoto.'''<ref name="國學院大學テシタルミューシアム-2021a" />
== Summary == He was born out of a kami-making competition between Amaterasu and [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]].
In many versions, Susanoo took Amaterasu's beads and crushed them within his mouth, which created five male kami.<ref>{{cite book |author=Seigo Takahashi |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=H18tAAAAYAAJ&dq=Amaterasu+and+Susanoo+oath&pg=PA66 |title=A Study of the Origin of the Japanese State |publisher=W. D. Gray |year=1917}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Amenooshihomimi • A History of Japan - 日本歴史 |url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/amenooshihomimi/ |access-date=2020-09-28 |website=A History of Japan - 日本歴史 |language=en-GB}}</ref> The first one to be born was {{ill|Ame-no-oshihomimi|en|Ame-no-oshihomimi|qid=Q462555|lt=Amenooshihomimi|1=Ame-no-oshihomimi}}, second was [[Ame no Hohi]], third was [[Amatsuhikone]], fourth was {{ill|Ikutsuhikone|en|Ikutsuhikone|qid=Q11147583|lt=Ikutsuhikone|1=Ikutsuhikone}}, and [[Kumanokusubi]] was the fifth.<ref>{{Cite book |last1=Havens |first1=Norman |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a_YQAQAAIAAJ&q=Amenooshihomimi |title=An Encyclopedia of Shinto (Shinto Jiten): Kami |last2=Inoue |first2=Nobutaka |date=2006 |publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics Kokugakuin University |isbn=978-4-905853-08-4 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Shinto Portal - IJCC, Kokugakuin University |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=33}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |year=1990 |title=The Journal of the Association of Teachers of Japanese |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Q5cqAQAAIAAJ&q=Amatsuhikone}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Kumanokusubi |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=99 |access-date=2020-10-07 |website=eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp}}</ref>
